However, existing IDP algorithms induce a critical utility imbalance problem: Data from owners with stronger privacy requirements may be severely underrepresented in the trained model, resulting in poorer performance on similar data from subsequent users during deployment. In this paper, we analyze this problem and propose the INO-SGD algorithm, which strategically down-weights data within each batch to improve performance on the more private data across all iterations. Notably, our algorithm is specially designed to satisfy IDP, while existing techniques addressing utility imbalance neither satisfy IDP nor can be easily adapted to do so. Lastly, we demonstrate the empirical feasibility of our approach.

</details>


### [586] [Prototype Guided Post-pretraining for Single-Cell Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07938)
*Sachini Weerasekara,Natasha Darras,Sagar Kamarthi,Colles Price,Jacqueline Isaacs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellRefine is a novel intermediary method introduced for single-cell representation learning (SCRL) that refines embeddings between pretraining and fine-tuning stages, leveraging marker-gene sets to improve model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-cell pretrained models are limited by cell-type distribution imbalances and struggle with generalizing under shifts in gene expression data. Fine-tuning alone fails to fully address these limitations.

Method: CellRefine introduces a post-pretraining step with a multi-faceted objective that integrates marker-gene sets as structural priors to refine latent embeddings of cells.

Result: Empirical experiments show that CellRefine enhances SCRL performance across multiple tasks, achieving up to 15% improvement in results.

Conclusion: CellRefine improves the generalizability and downstream task performance in single-cell foundation models, addressing fundamental limitations posed by cell-type distributions and covariate shifts.

Abstract: Single-cell representation learning (SCRL) from gene expression data offers a way to uncover the complex regulatory logic underlying cellular function. Inspired by large language models in natural language modeling, several single-cell pretrained models have recently been proposed that treat genes as tokens and cells as sentences. However, these models are fundamentally limited by the long-tailed nature of cell-type distributions and struggle to generalize under covariate shifts in gene expression data. While fine-tuning is often used to mitigate these issues, we observe that performance remains bounded. To address this challenge, we introduce CellRefine, a post-pretraining method that operates between the pretraining and fine-tuning stages of a single-cell foundation model. CellRefine uses a multi-faceted objective that incorporates marker-gene sets as structural priors to guide post-pretraining and refine the latent embedding manifold of cells. Across multiple computational biology tasks, empirical results show that CellRefine consistently improves downstream performance, yielding gains up to 15%.

</details>


### [587] [Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics: Theory and Applications to Training-Free Guided Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07950)
*Atsushi Nitanda,Dake Bu,Yueming Lyu,Tanya Veeravalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics (SALD) and its variant Velocity-Aware SALD (VA-SALD) for efficiently tracking paths of target distributions with convergence guarantees and applications in training-free guided generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to develop sampling methods with convergence guarantees for tracking moving target distributions and to address challenges in training-free guided generation using pretrained score-based generative models.

Method: The paper uses a KL differential inequality framework to study convergence and introduces VA-SALD, which accounts for the dynamics of pretrained models and corrects deviations caused by guidance, improving generation quality.

Result: SALD and VA-SALD demonstrate improved tracking of target distributions and principled training-free guided generation capabilities for generative model families while providing theoretical convergence guarantees.

Conclusion: The proposed SALD and VA-SALD frameworks advance sampling methodologies, offering theoretical insights and practical implications for training-free guided generation in generative models.

Abstract: We study Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics (SALD), a sampler for tracking a path of moving target distributions and approximating the terminal target through time slowdown. We establish non-asymptotic convergence guarantees via a KL differential inequality, showing that slowdown improves tracking through contraction of intermediate targets and the complexity of the path. Motivated by training-free guided generation with pretrained score-based generative models, we further introduce Velocity-Aware SALD (VA-SALD), which explicitly incorporates the underlying marginal distributions of the pretrained model and uses slowdown to correct the additional deviation induced by guidance. This yields a principled framework for training-free guided generation for diffusion-based and related generative model families, together with convergence guarantees that clarify the roles of intermediate functional inequalities and guidance bias. Code is available at https://github.com/anitan0925/sald.

</details>


### [588] [Convergent Stochastic Training of Attention and Understanding LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07959)
*Zhengkai Sun,Dibyakanti Kumar,Alejandro F Frangi,Anirbit Mukherjee,Mingfei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves trainability of models using attention layers and LoRA in a unified framework, providing theoretical guarantees under stochastic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the theoretical foundations and trainability of attention layers and Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA) models under stochastic optimization methods.

Method: The authors utilize a unified framework to establish that certain stochastic dynamics (mimicking SGD) minimize regression losses. They link empirical loss regularization to Poincaré inequality of Gibbs' measures.

Result: They rigorously demonstrate trainability for attention layers and LoRA without assumptions on data or architecture size.

Conclusion: This work provides foundational theoretical guarantees for the effectiveness of attention layers and LoRA models under practical stochastic training conditions.

Abstract: Transformers have revolutionized machine learning and deploying attention layers in the model is increasingly standard across a myriad of applications. Further, for large models, it is common to implement Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA), whereby a factorized parameterization of them is trained, to achieve a surprisingly beneficial accuracy-size trade-off. In this work, via a unified framework we rigorously establish trainability of such models under stochastic methods. We prove that for any mild regularization, the empirical regression loss on a attention layer and LoRA on a shallow neural net, both induce Poincaré inequality for the corresponding Gibbs' measure. Then it follows via invoking recent results that a certain SDE, which mimics the SGD, minimizes the corresponding losses. In both the cases, our first-of-its-kind results of trainability on attention and nets, do not rely on any assumptions on the data or the size of the architecture.

</details>


### [589] [Graph Representation Learning Augmented Model Manipulation on Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07961)
*Hanlin Cai,Kai Li,Houtianfu Wang,Haofan Dong,Yichen Li,Falko Dressler,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel attack strategy, AugMP, designed to compromise Federated Fine-Tuning (FFT) of large language models, achieving high manipulation effectiveness while evading defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated Fine-Tuning enables privacy-preserving collaboration on LLMs but faces model manipulation threats, where adversarial agents degrade global model performance, necessitating more advanced attack and defense insights.

Method: The authors propose AugMP, leveraging graph representation learning to analyze benign LLM updates, and applying an iterative augmented Lagrangian dual optimization method to generate stealthy malicious updates that mimic benign characteristics.

Result: Experiments show AugMP outperforms existing baselines, reducing global LLM accuracy by 26% and local LLM agents' average accuracy by 22%, while exhibiting high similarity to benign updates, evading defenses.

Conclusion: The AugMP strategy effectively highlights vulnerabilities in FFT frameworks, pushing the need for improved robust mechanisms against manipulation attacks in federated learning of LLMs.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) has emerged as a privacy-preserving paradigm for collaboratively adapting large language models (LLMs). Built upon federated learning, FFT enables distributed agents to jointly refine a shared pretrained LLM by aggregating local LLM updates without sharing local raw data. However, FFT-based LLMs remain vulnerable to model manipulation threats, in which adversarial participants upload manipulated LLM updates that corrupt the aggregation process and degrade the performance of the global LLM. In this paper, we propose an Augmented Model maniPulation (AugMP) strategy against FFT-based LLMs. Specifically, we design a novel graph representation learning framework that captures feature correlations among benign LLM updates to guide the generation of malicious updates. To enhance manipulation effectiveness and stealthiness, we develop an iterative manipulation algorithm based on an augmented Lagrangian dual formulation. Through this formulation, malicious updates are optimized to embed adversarial objectives while preserving benign-like parameter characteristics. Experimental results across multiple LLM backbones demonstrate that the AugMP strategy achieves the strongest manipulation performance among all competing baselines, reducing the global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and degrading the average accuracy of local LLM agents by up to 22%. Meanwhile, AugMP maintains high statistical and geometric consistency with benign updates, enabling it to evade conventional distance- and similarity-based defense methods.

</details>


### [590] [Aggregation in conformal e-classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07963)
*Vladimir Vovk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores a technique for aggregating conformal e-predictors to improve predictive efficiency without compromising validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to enhance the aggregation of conformal e-predictors, which have computational advantages and maintain prediction validity.

Method: Researchers evaluate cross-conformal e-prediction experimentally, along with simplified and flexible modifications.

Result: The study provides insights into the effectiveness of the proposed simplified aggregation methods.

Conclusion: Simpler and flexible aggregation methods for conformal e-predictors can improve practicality and performance while retaining validity.

Abstract: Aggregating conformal predictors is a standard way of balancing their predictive and computational efficiency while retaining their validity, at least approximately. An important advantage of conformal e-predictors is that they are easier to aggregate without sacrificing their validity. This paper studies experimentally cross-conformal e-prediction, which is an existing method of aggregating conformal e-predictors, and its modifications that are conceptually simpler and more flexible.

</details>


### [591] [When Diffusion Model Can Ignore Dimension: An Entropy-Based Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07969)
*Ahmad Aghapour,Erhan Bayraktar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the efficiency of diffusion models in high-dimensional spaces, proposing an information-theoretic lens tied to Shannon entropy instead of dimensionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why diffusion models perform well in high dimensions despite prior theories suggesting dimensional inefficiencies.

Method: The authors analyze convergence behavior in Gaussian mixture targets and discrete distributions, linking sampler efficiency to Shannon entropy and compact latent representations.

Result: Discretization error scales with latent entropy rather than ambient dimension, suggesting linear complexity in entropy and logarithmic dependence on data's second moment.

Conclusion: Diffusion sampling is efficient in high-dimensional spaces if the distribution has compact latent representations, explaining its performance in natural image contexts.

Abstract: Diffusion models perform remarkably well on high-dimensional data such as images, often using only a modest number of reverse-time steps. Despite this practical success, existing convergence theory does not fully explain why such samplers remain efficient in high dimensions. Many prior KL guarantees bound the discretization error in terms of the ambient dimension, while other improved results replace this dependence using intrinsic-dimensional or geometric structure assumptions. In this work, we develop an alternative information-theoretic perspective on diffusion sampler convergence. We prove that, for Gaussian mixture targets, the discretization error is controlled by the Shannon entropy of the latent mixture component rather than by the ambient dimension. Consequently, the leading step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy and depends only logarithmically on the second moment of the data. Our analysis also extends to discrete target distributions, where the relevant complexity is the entropy of the target rather than the dimension of the embedding space. These results suggest that diffusion sampling can remain efficient in high-dimensional spaces when the data distribution admits a compact latent representation, as is widely believed to be the case for natural images.

</details>


### [592] [Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement in Online Federated LLM Fine-Tuning with Real-Time Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07977)
*Seohyun Lee,Wenzhi Fang,Dong-Jun Han,Seyyedali Hosseinalipour,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SPEAR, an efficient online learning algorithm for enhancing federated large language model (LLM) fine-tuning using feedback-guided self-play loops.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The limitations of existing feedback-based learning systems, which rely on offline setups and privileged ground-truth contexts, and the need for efficient methods in federated learning for resource-constrained edge devices.

Method: SPEAR uses feedback-guided self-play to generate contrastive pairs per prompt and trains on maximum likelihood for correct completions and confidence-weighted unlikelihood for incorrect completions.

Result: SPEAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on various benchmark datasets and showcases its ability to train efficiently without ground-truth contexts.

Conclusion: SPEAR enables efficient online federated learning model fine-tuning through innovative feedback-based techniques, making it accessible for resource-limited environments.

Abstract: Recent works have advanced feedback-based learning systems, whereby a foundation model is able to intake incoming feedback (e.g., a user) to self-improve, creating a self-loop system of training. However, existing works are limited in needing to consider an offline setup to allow for such feedback-based methods, and are further limited in the need of requiring privileged ground-truth contexts for training. Moreover, there is limited consideration of federated learning (FL), which is particularly well-suited for incorporating external feedback across large networks of end users, for example, but requires methods to be efficient for training on resource-constrained edge devices. Therefore, we introduce SPEAR (Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement), an efficient online learning algorithm for federated LLM fine-tuning. SPEAR utilizes a feedback-guided self-play loop to construct naturally contrastive pairs per prompt which are utilized to be trained on (i) standard maximum likelihood on correct completions and (ii) confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions. Without the need of expensive group generations and ground-truth contexts for training (i.e., only partial, non-answer feedback), in contrast with existing works, SPEAR can be trained both online and in a resource-efficient manner. We validate SPEAR across various benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superior performance in comparison to state-of-the-art baselines. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/lee3296/SPEAR.

</details>


### [593] [Susceptibilities and Patterning: A Primer on Linear Response in Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07980)
*Chris Elliott,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends the theory of susceptibilities to interpret neural networks, linking data perturbations to posterior expectations and providing tools for analyzing model-data interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how neural networks respond to data perturbations and connecting this to a theoretical framework derived from statistical mechanics.

Method: Defines susceptibilities using posterior expectations and covariance, introduces the susceptibility and influence matrices, and links the concepts to the geometry of the loss landscape.

Result: Establishes a connection between susceptibility theory and tools like the Bayesian influence function, structural susceptibility matrix, and patterning problems.

Conclusion: The framework offers a linearized approach to understanding and influencing neural network behavior, applicable to loss landscape analysis and model-data interactions.

Abstract: These notes introduce the theory of susceptibilities as developed in [arXiv:2504.18274, arXiv:2601.12703] for interpreting neural networks. The susceptibility of an observable $φ$ to a data perturbation is defined as a derivative of a posterior expectation, which by the fluctuation--dissipation theorem equals a posterior covariance. Different choices of $φ$ yield different objects: per-sample losses give the influence matrix (the Bayesian influence function of [arXiv:2509.26544]), while component-localized observables give the structural susceptibility matrix that pairs model components with data patterns. The susceptibility matrix is (up to a factor of $nβ$) the Jacobian of the map from data distributions to structural coordinates; its pseudo-inverse provides a linearized solution to the patterning problem of [arXiv:2601.13548]: finding data perturbations that produce a desired structural change. We motivate the theory from its statistical-mechanical foundations, then give a detailed exposition of susceptibilities, their empirical estimators, and their connection to the geometry of the loss landscape.

</details>


### [594] [Where's the Plan? Locating Latent Planning in Language Models with Lightweight Mechanistic Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07984)
*Nicole Ma,Nick Rui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study examines whether language models encode and utilize information about structurally-constrained future content, such as rhyming patterns, within their internal processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how, when, and where language models encode and utilize information about future token constraints (like rhyming) to improve their generation capabilities.

Method: The study uses linear probing and activation patching on three language model families (Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3) across multiple scales to detect and analyze future token encoding and causal drivers of token generation.

Result: All tested models encode future rhyme information at the line boundary, improving with model scale. However, only Gemma-3-27B causally utilizes this information, involving a transition from rhyme-word reliance to line boundary reliance around layer 30. Specific attention heads were found responsible for this capability.

Conclusion: Future constraint information, such as rhyming, is encoded in language models, but its causal impact on generation is limited to specific architectures like Gemma-3-27B, where targeted attention heads enable this capability.

Abstract: We study planning site formation in language models -- where internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens form during the forward pass, and whether they causally drive generation. Using rhyming-couplet completion as a clean test of forward-looking constraint, we apply two lightweight methods (linear probing and activation patching) across Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 at more than ten scales. Probing shows that future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary, with signal that strengthens with scale in all three families. Activation patching reveals that only Gemma-3-27B causally relies on this encoding, exhibiting a handoff in which the causal driver migrates from the rhyme word to the line boundary around layer 30. Every other model we test conditions on the rhyme word throughout generation, with near-zero causal effect at the line boundary despite strong probe signal. We localize the Gemma-3-27B handoff to five attention heads through two-stage path patching that recover ~90% of the rhyme-routing capacity at the newline.

</details>


### [595] [Bayesian Sensitivity of Causal Inference Estimators under Evidence-Based Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07993)
*Nikita Dhawan,Daniel Shen,Leonardo Cotta,Chris J. Maddison*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper critiques existing sensitivity analysis frameworks in causal inference for being overly pessimistic and introduces a Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) framework using priors based on real-world evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal inference in observational studies depends heavily on untestable assumptions, but current sensitivity analysis frameworks often yield overly pessimistic or unrealistic conclusions, contradicting prior real-world knowledge.

Method: This study generalizes an existing s-value framework to analyze sensitivity of causal assumptions and develops a Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) using Monte Carlo approximations to consider realistic priors.

Result: The authors find existing worst-case sensitivity analyses rely on unrealistic assumptions and demonstrate the utility of the BSV criterion in a diabetes observational study.

Conclusion: The proposed BSV framework offers a more informative and realistic approach to sensitivity analysis by incorporating prior knowledge, improving robustness and applicability in causal inference.

Abstract: Causal inference, especially in observational studies, relies on untestable assumptions about the true data-generating process. Sensitivity analysis helps us determine how robust our conclusions are when we alter these underlying assumptions. Existing frameworks for sensitivity analysis are concerned with worst-case changes in assumptions. In this work, we argue that using such pessimistic criteria can often become uninformative or lead to conclusions contradicting our prior knowledge about the world. To demonstrate this claim, we generalize the recent s-value framework (Gupta & Rothenhäusler, 2023) to estimate the sensitivity of three different common assumptions in causal inference. Empirically, we find that, indeed, worst-case conclusions about sensitivity can rely on unrealistic changes in the data-generating process. To overcome this, we extend the s-value framework with a new sensitivity analysis criterion: Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV), which computes the expected sensitivity of an estimate to assumption violations under priors constructed from real-world evidence. We use Monte Carlo approximations to estimate this quantity and illustrate its applicability in an observational study on the effect of diabetes treatments on weight loss.

</details>


### [596] [Graph-Structured Hyperdimensional Computing for Data-Efficient and Explainable Process-Structure-Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07999)
*Jingzhan Ge,Ajeeth Vellore,Ajinkya Palwe,Ahsan Khan,David Gorsich,Matthew P. Castanier,SeungYeon Kang,Farhad Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel framework, PSP-HDC, which improves predictions for 3D microstructure fabrication using multiphoton photoreduction by encoding process-structure-property (PSP) relationships graphically in hyperdimensional computing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current challenges in predicting the PSP relationships during multiphoton photoreduction include sparse and heterogeneous data dominated by interdependencies. Existing models either lack statistical reliability or require unavailable calibrated submodels during early development, necessitating a new approach.

Method: The PSP-HDC framework leverages a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing approach. It uses a scalar-to-hypervector encoder to embed parameters, binds them along PSP graph dependencies, and makes predictions via associative-memory retrieval against class prototypes. Unified prototypes facilitate both prediction and intrinsic explanations.

Result: PSP-HDC achieves high accuracy (0.910 +/- 0.077) on sheet-resistance regime prediction, even under challenging process-fold generalization (0.896), surpassing existing models.

Conclusion: PSP-HDC offers a robust, interpretable, and scalable solution for PSP prediction in complex 3D microfabrication processes, enabling better prediction and explanation of results compared to traditional models.

Abstract: Multiphoton photoreduction enables high-fidelity fabrication of complex 3D microstructures, yet reliable process-structure-property (PSP) prediction remains difficult because the available data are sparse, heterogeneous, and interaction-dominated. In this regime, conventional feature-vector models are statistically underdetermined, making them prone to spurious correlations, poor regime transfer, and unstable post hoc explanations, whereas mechanistic pipelines depend on calibrated submodels that are rarely available during early process development. We present PSP-HDC, a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing framework that encodes a directed PSP graph as an internal prior for representation, inference, and explanation. A trainable scalar-to-hypervector encoder learns parameter-specific embeddings on a fixed hyperdimensional basis to accommodate heterogeneous scales and noise. Sample representations are then composed through graph-aligned binding and bundling along directed PSP dependencies, and prediction is performed by associative-memory retrieval against class prototypes. Because the same prototype memories support both decision making and attribution, PSP-HDC provides intrinsic explanations at the parameter, group, and within-group levels, while memory alignment and separation quantify prototype formation during training. On sheet-resistance regime prediction for the 3D platform, PSP-HDC achieves an accuracy of 0.910 +/- 0.077 over 1000 random splits and 0.896 under process-fold generalization, outperforming strong baselines.

</details>


### [597] [STEPS: A Temporal Smooth Error Propagation Solver on the Manifolds for Test-Time Adaptation in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08005)
*Jiaqi Liu,Yifan Ouyang,Zhifei Song,Sim Kuan Goh,Ashwaq Qasem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces STEPS, a method to handle test-time adaptation (TTA) in time-series forecasting under distribution shifts and data constraints. STEPS reformulates the problem as a Dirichlet Boundary Value Problem to address key challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve forecasting models under challenging test-time scenarios where observations are limited, noisy, or sparse, tackling weak identifiability, error accumulation, and unstable corrections.

Method: STEPS uses a Local Solver, a Global Solver, and Spatiotemporal Manifold Fusion (SMF) to propagate errors and achieve smooth and bounded corrections in prediction space.

Result: STEPS reduces average relative MSE by 26.82% compared to zero-shot models and outperforms TTA baselines by 12.77% across multiple benchmarks and scenarios.

Conclusion: STEPS effectively enhances the robustness and accuracy of test-time forecasting adaptation under sparse and noisy observation settings.

Abstract: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to improve time series forecasting under distribution shifts by using limited observations revealed during inference. However, forecasting TTA must operate in a source-free online setting, where the adaptation signal is short, temporally correlated, and potentially noisy. Existing methods can therefore suffer from weak identifiability, error accumulation, and unstable long-horizon corrections when the revealed prefix is sparse or contaminated. To address these issues, we propose STEPS, a Smooth Temporal Error Propagation Solver for TTA in time-series forecasting. STEPS reformulates forecasting TTA as a Dirichlet Boundary Value Problem on a temporal manifold, where the revealed prefix error serves as the boundary condition for the unknown future error field. Then, STEPS solves a smooth and bounded correction field in prediction space: a Local Solver propagates prefix errors under temporal smoothness, a Global Solver retrieves stable cross-window error memory and Spatiotemporal Manifold Fusion (SMF) integrates both solutions into the final correction. Across six standard benchmarks and four frozen backbones, STEPS achieves an average relative MSE reduction of 26.82% over the zero-shot backbone, exceeding the strongest compared TTA baseline by 12.77%. Additional sparse prefix and contamination tests confirm the robustness of STEPS under limited and noisy prefixes.

</details>


### [598] [Interpreting Reinforcement Learning Agents with Susceptibilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08007)
*Chris Elliott,Einar Urdshals,David Quarel,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Susceptibilities, a technique for neural network interpretability, have been extended to study regret in deep reinforcement learning (DRL), demonstrating their utility in revealing training dynamics beyond learned policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gain deeper interpretability of neural networks by understanding internal training dynamics in deep reinforcement learning, especially through the lens of regret instead of just policy outcomes.

Method: The paper generalizes susceptibilities applied to posterior expectations in supervised settings and adapts them to analyze regret in DRL. A simple gridworld model with defined stages of learning development was used, complemented by activation-steering and thoughts on post-training RLHF extensions.

Result: Susceptibilities provided insights into the internal parameter-space development in DRL that are not detectable via policy progression alone. This was validated using activation-steering analysis.

Conclusion: Susceptibilities offer a valuable tool for understanding the internal learning dynamics in DRL and potentially support post-training interventions such as RLHF.

Abstract: Susceptibilities are a technique for neural network interpretability that studies the response of posterior expectation values of observables to perturbations of the loss. We generalize this construction to the setting of the regret in deep reinforcement learning and investigate the utility of susceptibilities in a simple gridworld model that nevertheless exhibits non-trivial stagewise development. We argue that susceptibilities reveal internal features of the development of the model in parameter space that one cannot detect purely by studying the development of the learned policy. We validate these results with activation-steering, and discuss the framework's extension to RLHF post-training.

</details>


### [599] [Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Traffic State Estimation with Sparse Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08028)
*Eunhan Ka,Ludovic Leclercq,Satish V. Ukkusuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes ADD-PINN, a method that improves traffic state estimation by using adaptive domain decomposition to handle shockwaves in traffic models effectively. It outperforms other baselines in terms of accuracy and efficiency in reconstructing traffic speed fields from sparse sensor data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of reconstructing the traffic state from sparse fixed sensors, where traditional PINNs tend to over-smooth shocks in traffic models.

Method: ADD-PINN uses a two-stage framework: training a global PINN followed by residual-guided decomposition to define subdomain boundaries, supported by a data-driven shock indicator for single-domain fallback.

Result: ADD-PINN consistently yields lower error rates in traffic reconstruction compared to neural and PINN-based baselines, with faster training time and improved handling of sparse sensing scenarios. Negative control experiments confirm its reliability in non-decomposable conditions.

Conclusion: Residual-guided adaptive spatial decomposition in ADD-PINN provides an effective and efficient approach for offline traffic reconstruction, especially in conditions of sparse sensing and localized transitions.

Abstract: Traffic state estimation from sparse fixed sensors is challenging because physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) tend to over-smooth the shockwaves admitted by the Lighthill-Whitham-Richards (LWR) model. This study proposes Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks (ADD-PINN), a two-stage residual-guided framework for LWR-based offline speed-field reconstruction. A coarse global PINN is first trained; its spatial residual profile is then used to place subdomain boundaries and initialize child subnetworks in a decomposition-enabled mode, while a data-driven shock indicator can retain a single-domain fallback when localized evidence of transition is weak. The primary offline I-24 MOTION evaluation spans five days, five sensor configurations, and ten seeds per configuration, yielding 1,500 runs in total. Against neural and physics-informed baselines, ADD-PINN attains the lowest relative L2 error in 18 of 25 configurations and in 14 of 15 sparse-sensing cases, while training 2.4 times faster than the extended PINN (XPINN) baseline. An ablation study supports spatial-only decomposition as an effective default for fixed-sensor traffic reconstruction in the evaluated settings. Supplementary Next Generation Simulation (NGSIM) experiments serve as a negative control: the shock indicator suppresses decomposition in all 50 runs, and the default single-domain fallback ranks first across all sensor configurations. These results support residual-guided spatial decomposition as an effective PINN-family design for offline reconstruction when sparse fixed sensing coincides with localized transition regions.

</details>


### [600] [Don't Get Your Kroneckers in a Twist: Gaussian Processes on High-Dimensional Incomplete Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08036)
*Mads Greisen Højlund,August Smart Lykke-Møller,Henry Moss,Ove Christiansen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents CUTS-GPR, a method for scalable and numerically exact Gaussian process regression (GPR) in high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the computational challenges of performing Gaussian process regression on large datasets with high dimensionality, particularly for applications like modeling high-dimensional potential energy surfaces.

Method: The method involves combining an additive kernel with an incomplete grid to create a fast kernel matrix-vector product. This structure enables near-linear to linear scaling with the number of data points and low-order polynomial scaling with dimensionality.

Result: CUTS-GPR achieves benchmarks on data with billions of points and dimensions in the thousands, completing full GPR tasks, including hyperparameter optimization, in hours for large-scale datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed approach makes Bayesian modeling feasible for high-dimensional tasks and addresses computational limits in areas such as computational chemistry.

Abstract: We introduce CUTS-GPR, a new method for performing numerically exact Gaussian process regression (GPR) in high-dimensional settings. The key component of CUTS-GPR is an extremely fast kernel matrix-vector product, which exhibits near-linear or even linear scaling with the amount of training data, $N$, and low-order polynomial scaling with dimensionality, $D$. This is obtained by combining an additive kernel with an incomplete grid and exploiting the resulting structure of the kernel matrix. We demonstrate the scalability of the matrix-vector product by running benchmarks with billions of data points and thousands of dimensions. Full GPR calculations, including hyperparameter optimization, are completed in a matter of hours for $N = 447 265$ and $D = 24$. We demonstrate that our CUTS-GPR enables Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces - a longstanding challenge in computational chemistry.

</details>


### [601] [Beyond Pairs: Your Language Model is Secretly Optimizing a Preference Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08037)
*Ning Liu,Chuanneng Sun,Kristina Klinkner,Shervin Malmasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Graph Direct Preference Optimization (GraphDPO), a method that extends pairwise Direct Preference Optimization to leverage richer preference structures via graphs, improving language model alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address limitations of pairwise Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) which fails to fully utilize rich preference structures when training on data with multiple rollouts per prompt, resulting in unstable optimization and redundant supervision.

Method: The authors propose GraphDPO, a method that uses directed acyclic preference graphs to encode dominance relations among rollouts, employs a graph-structured Plackett-Luce-inspired objective, and introduces equivalence-class constructions to handle sparse signals. It also includes optional ground-truth anchoring for more stable training.

Result: GraphDPO shows improved performance in reasoning and program synthesis tasks, demonstrating that graph-structured preference modeling is scalable and robust.

Conclusion: GraphDPO provides a principled and scalable alternative to pairwise and listwise preference alignment, efficiently leveraging richer data structures for better language model training.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) aligns language models using pairwise preference comparisons, offering a simple and effective alternative to Reinforcement Learning (RL) from human feedback. However, in many practical settings, training data consists of multiple rollouts per prompt, inducing rich preference structure that pairwise DPO fails to exploit. Collapsing such data into independent pairs discards transitivity, introduces redundant or conflicting supervision, and can lead to unstable optimization. We propose Graph Direct Preference Optimization (GraphDPO), a principled generalization of DPO that operates over directed acyclic preference graphs induced by rollout rankings. GraphDPO encodes dominance relations as edges and optimizes a graph-structured Plackett--Luce-inspired objective that aggregates supervision over graph neighborhoods, enforcing transitivity while recovering standard DPO as a special case. To handle discrete or sparse signals, we introduce an equivalence-class construction where responses with identical preferences form graph layers, and intra-layer edges contribute zero loss, preventing spurious gradients. Despite leveraging full graph structure, GraphDPO maintains linear per-prompt complexity via efficient log-sum-exp aggregation. We further incorporate optional ground-truth anchoring by inserting verified solutions as dominant nodes and applying an annealed schedule that stabilizes early training while gradually relaxing oracle supervision. Experiments on reasoning and program synthesis tasks demonstrate superior performance, suggesting that graph-structured preference modeling is a scalable and robust alternative to pairwise and listwise alignment objectives.

</details>


### [602] [Reinforcement Learning for Exponential Utility: Algorithms and Convergence in Discounted MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08053)
*Gugan Thoppe,L. A. Prashanth,Ankur Naskar,Sanjay Bhat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the gap in reinforcement learning algorithms for exponential-utility optimization in discounted Markov decision processes. It proposes two Q-value-style extensions, proves their optimality, and develops two model-free algorithms, establishing their convergence properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in principled algorithms for reinforcement learning focused on exponential-utility optimization in Markov decision processes.

Method: The researchers derive Q-value-style extensions for exponential utility, characterize their fixed points, and develop two model-free RL algorithms. They prove convergence using techniques such as timescale separation and local Lipschitzness.

Result: Two model-free algorithms are introduced: a two-timescale Q-learning algorithm and a one-timescale sublinear power-law operator algorithm. Both approaches are proven to converge, though finite-time convergence rates differ.

Conclusion: This study establishes foundational methods for value-based reinforcement learning under exponential-utility objectives, offering convergence guarantees and expanding the theoretical understanding of utility-focused RL.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for exponential-utility optimization in discounted Markov decision processes (MDPs) lacks principled value-based algorithms. We address this gap in the fixed risk-aversion setting. Building on the Bellman-type equation for exponential utility studied in \cite{porteus1975optimality}, we derive two Q-value-style extensions and show that the associated operators are contractions in the $L_\infty$ and sup-log/Thompson metrics, respectively. We characterize their fixed points and prove that the induced greedy stationary policy is optimal for the exponential-utility objective among stationary policies. These structural results lead to two model-free algorithms: a two-timescale Q-learning--style algorithm, for which we establish almost-sure convergence and provide finite-time convergence rates via timescale separation, and a one-timescale algorithm governed by a sublinear power-law operator. Since the latter does not admit a global contraction in standard metrics, we prove its convergence using delicate arguments based on local Lipschitzness, monotonicity, homogeneity, and Dini derivatives, and provide a scalar finite-time analysis that highlights the challenges in obtaining convergence rates in the vector case. Our work provides a foundation for value-based RL under exponential-utility objectives.

</details>


### [603] [GRAPHLCP: Structure-Aware Localized Conformal Prediction on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08074)
*Peyman Baghershahi,Fangxin Wang,Debmalya Mandal,Sourav Medya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GRAPHLCP, a conformal prediction framework for graph-based data, optimizing uncertainty quantification by incorporating graph topology and node dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification in graph neural networks is challenging due to graph combinatorial complexity, leading to unreliable predictions and embeddings.

Method: GRAPHLCP enhances standard conformal prediction with topology-aware anchor sampling, calibration weighting, and feature-aware densification for graph-based uncertainty quantification.

Result: Tests on regression and classification datasets show GRAPHLCP maintains finite-sample guarantees while improving prediction conditional coverage.

Conclusion: GRAPHLCP effectively integrates graph structure into conformal prediction, advancing uncertainty quantification for graph-based learning models.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides a distribution-free approach to uncertainty quantification with finite-sample guarantees. However, applying CP to graph neural networks (GNNs) remains challenging as the combinatorial nature of graphs often leads to insufficiently certain predictions and indiscriminative embeddings. Existing methods primarily rely on embedding-space proximity for localization, which can be unreliable for graphs and yield inefficient prediction sets. We propose GRAPHLCP, a proximity-based localized CP framework that explicitly incorporates graph topology and inter-node dependencies into localization and weighting. Our approach introduces a feature-aware densification step to mitigate locality bias in sparse graphs, followed by a Personalized PageRank-based kernel computation to model structural proximity. This enables topology-dependent anchor sampling and calibration weighting that captures both local and long-range dependencies. Extensive experiments on several regression and classification datasets demonstrate that GRAPHLCP guarantees marginal coverage with finite samples while efficiently attaining favorable test conditional coverage across various conditioning scenarios.

</details>


### [604] [Zero-Shot Imagined Speech Decoding via Imagined-to-Listened MEG Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08075)
*Maryam Maghsoudi,Shihab Shamma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to decode imagined speech using MEG recordings paired with listened speech recordings, showing promising above-chance decoding with scalable potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoding imagined speech from brain recordings is hard due to scarce and temporally misaligned data across subjects. This work seeks to improve decoding by leveraging paired listening and imagining datasets.

Method: The study used a three-stage decoding pipeline. Stage one mapped imagined MEG responses to listened MEG responses using six linear and neural models. Stage two trained a word decoder on listened data. Stage three processed imagined brain responses to generate corresponding listened responses, validated through the trained decoder.

Result: Imagined speech decoding was achieved above chance levels. The results also indicated that performance improved with more training data, demonstrating scalability and potential for brain-computer interfaces.

Conclusion: This proof-of-concept suggests that decoding imagined speech is feasible by leveraging richer datasets from listened speech and shows promise for real-world brain-computer interface applications.

Abstract: Decoding imagined speech from non-invasive brain recordings is challenging because imagined datasets are scarce and difficult to align temporally across subjects and sessions In this work, we propose a new approach to the decoding of imagined speech that leverages the richer and more reliably labeled recordings during listening to speech. We collected paired listened and imagined MEG recordings to rhythmic melodic and spoken stimuli from trained musicians. Using trained musicians helped improve temporal alignment across conditions. We then developed a three-stage decoding pipeline that revealed consistent and meaningful relationships between neural activity evoked by imagining and listening to the same stimuli. First, we trained six linear and neural models to map imagined MEG responses to listened responses. We evaluated these models against a null baseline from unseen subjects to validate that the predicted-listening responses preserve stimulus-specific information. In the second stage, we trained a contrastive word decoder exclusively on the listened MEG responses, and evaluated it using four embedding strategies including semantic, acoustic, and phonetic representations. In the third stage, we process the imagined MEG responses from held-out subjects through the mapping pipeline to compute the corresponding listening responses that are then decoded by the listened decoder. Using rank-based analysis, we show that the imagined words are decodable significantly above chance. We shall report here the results of a proof-of-concept implementation to decode imagined speech, where all evaluations are performed on held-out subjects. We also demonstrate that performance improves with training data size, suggesting that this approach is scalable and can directly be made applicable to realistic brain-computer interface scenarios.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [605] [The Causally Emergent Alignment Hypothesis: Causal Emergence Aligns with and Predicts Final Reward in Reinforcement Learning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06746)
*Federico Pigozzi,Michael Levin*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper explores causal emergence in artificial agents, particularly RL agents, and its connection to learning performance. Results highlight causal emergence as a potential driver of better RL agents and a bridge between biological and artificial learning mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While causal emergence has been observed in biological agents after learning, its presence and role in artificial agents—specifically RL agents—remains unclear. This study aims to fill that gap.

Method: The researchers analyzed RL agents with neural-network architectures across six increasingly complex environments. They measured causal emergence in the agents' latent-space representations over their lifetimes using ΦID and studied its relationship to learning outcomes.

Result: Findings suggest a 'Causally Emergent Alignment Hypothesis' where successful agents demonstrated causal emergence predictive of final rewards in early training. Additionally, causal emergence aligned with reward improvement dynamics in most tasks.

Conclusion: Causal emergence may represent an organizing principle in neural representations of RL agents, paralleling biological cognition. It offers new avenues for creating more effective and interpretable RL systems.

Abstract: A hallmark of life on Earth is the ability of agents to exert causal power and be drivers of subsequent events. This is key to cognition at all scales. Causal emergence, measuring the degree to which an agent exerts unique predictive power on its future, is one consequence of causal power. Indeed, recent discoveries have shown that biological agents, even minimal ones, increase their causal emergence after learning new memories. However, there is a major knowledge gap regarding how causally emergent artificial agents are. We focused on Reinforcement Learning (RL) of neural-network agents across an array of environmental conditions, encompassing different algorithms, agent architectures, and six environments arranged on a complexity spectrum. For consistency, we computed the causal emergence of their latent-space representations over their lifetimes. We used the recently proposed ΦID to estimate causal emergence and tested how it related to learning performance. Our results suggested a Causally Emergent Alignment Hypothesis: successful agents exhibited causal emergence that was consistently predictive of final reward early in training and whose representational dynamics aligned with reward improvement in most tasks. This idea suggests that causal emergence may be a previously undisclosed axis of reorganization of neural representations in RL agents, with the potential to establish causal relationships and interventions that will lead to better RL agents. Our work also highlights the alignment between causal emergence and learning as another way biological and artificial creatures compare.

</details>


### [606] [Direct-to-Event Spiking Neural Network Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07207)
*Nhan Trong Luu,Duong Trung Luu,Pham Ngoc Nam,Truong Cong Thang*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper addresses the transfer of direct-coded SNNs to energy-efficient event-based computation while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to make pretrained direct-coded SNNs reusable in energy-sensitive scenarios by converting them to more energy-efficient event-based representations.

Method: Authors performed a systematic investigation into the challenges of transition and proposed methods to enable effective transfer while preserving performance.

Result: Methods for converting direct-coded SNNs to event-based representations were developed, aiming for energy-efficiency and model performance preservation.

Conclusion: The research provides insights and methods for overcoming challenges in switching from direct-coded to event-based neuron implementations while ensuring energy-efficiency and functionality.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have gained increasing attention due to their potential for low-power computation on neuromorphic hardware. A widely adopted training strategy for SNNs is direct coding, which enable backpropagation on neuron implementations using continuous-valued surrogate activations. However, recent studies have shown that direct-coded SNNs remain substantially less energy-efficient than their event-based counterparts, limiting their practical deployment in energy sensitive scenarios. Still, to promote the reusability of pretrained SNN database on direct code, this motivates an important yet underexplored question: How can a SNN pretrained with direct code be effectively converted into an event-based representation? In this research, we present the first systematic investigation into this transfer problem, analyze the key challenges that arise when transitioning from direct-coded to event-based computation and propose a set of methods to enable energy-efficient transfer while preserving model performance.

</details>


### [607] [Broken-symmetry shape discrimination on a driven Duffing ring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07475)
*Kaspar Anton Schindler*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper explores fundamental operations on a cycle graph computational framework and analyzes its performance in representing and binding input signals, introducing robustness metrics and opportunities for further research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the interplay between two fundamental operations, bundling and binding, in distributed computational systems, specifically those based on cyclic substrates. This exploration seeks to reveal opportunities for improved signal processing and encoding methods.

Method: Using a cycle graph of N nodes, the paper tests a master equation in two regimes: a linear regime for feature representation and a Duffing regime for mode mixing. Methods include deriving and testing $φ_0$, an observable summarizing input responses under noise and dissipation.

Result: The linear regime performs comparably to the FFT baseline at high SNR, with advantages in transient, low SNR cases. The Duffing regime introduces sparse harmonic selection based on input shapes. The derived $φ_0$ observable proves robust against noise, maintaining informative content.

Conclusion: The paper confirms the potential of symmetry and dissipation-aware observables in encoding meaningful input responses. While $φ_0$ serves as a proof-of-concept, extending this framework to complex substrates and real-world signals remains a future direction.

Abstract: Distributed computational substrates rely on two elementary operations: bundling, the act of populating a shared physical medium with independently retrievable components, and binding, the act of composing components into outputs whose identity depends on their relations. We study these two primitives on the simplest closed substrate carrying a continuous symmetry, a cycle graph of N nodes, in two parameter regimes of a single master equation of motion. The linear regime sorts a temporal input across the substrate's U(1)-organised eigenmodes, providing a feature representation that matches a windowed-FFT baseline at high signal-to-noise ratio and modestly outperforms it for transient signals at low SNR. The Duffing regime activates a cubic mode-mixing operation constrained by the substrate's symmetry into a sparse selection rule on integer wavenumbers, generating shape-dependent harmonic content that the linear regime cannot produce. We identify a single-number observable, $φ_0$, that summarises the bound representation's response to input shape, and we analyse its symmetry structure: a $π$-periodicity in the shape parameter is exact, while a time-reversal symmetry that would render $φ_0$ degenerate is broken by the substrate's dissipation. The asymmetric status of these two symmetries is what licenses $φ_0$ as a meaningful single-number observable; its trajectory across the quotient domain encodes the joint response of binding and dissipation to the input shape. Numerical experiments confirm that $φ_0$ retains its information content under additive band-limited noise, with seed-averaged means staying clearly above the symmetric-attractor value down to 0 dB input SNR. The framework is developed on synthetic signals only; extensions to richer substrates, more elaborate drives, and real biological signals are open questions for the work that follows.

</details>


### [608] [Globally Optimal Training of Spiking Neural Networks via Parameter Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08022)
*Himanshu Udupi,Xiaocong Yang,ChengXiang Zhai*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper addresses training challenges in Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) by introducing a novel parameter reconstruction algorithm that outperforms traditional methods and combines effectively with surrogate gradient training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges of surrogate gradient methods introducing approximation errors during SNN training, and to improve scalability and robustness in large-scale SNN tasks.

Method: The authors extend convexification techniques from parallel feedforward to parallel recurrent threshold networks, building a theoretical framework for a new parameter reconstruction algorithm tailored for SNNs.

Result: The proposed algorithm shows consistent and significant advantages over traditional surrogate-gradient methods in various tasks, while also exhibiting scalability and robustness to diverse data and model configurations.

Conclusion: This work presents a promising approach to SNN training that could pave the way for more scalable and efficient applications in the large-scale settings.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have been proposed as biologically plausible and energy-efficient alternatives to conventional Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs). However, the training of SNN usually relies on surrogate gradients due to the non-differentiability of the spike function, introducing approximation errors that accumulate across layers. To address this challenge, we extend the work on convexification of parallel feedforward threshold networks to parallel recurrent threshold networks, which subsume parallel SNNs as a structured special case. Building on this theoretical framework, we propose a parameter reconstruction algorithm for SNN training that demonstrates consistent and significant advantages across various tasks, both as a standalone method and in combination with surrogate-gradient training. The ablations further demonstrate the data scalability and robustness to model configurations of our training algorithm, pointing toward its potential in large-scale SNN training.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [609] [Bounding Fixed Points of Non-Monotone Processes: Theory to Practice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06803)
*Abdullah H. Rasheed,Vijay K. Garg*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper addresses inefficiencies and coarse results in Approximation Fixpoint Theory (AFT) for non-monotone reasoning, proposing efficient and practical algorithms for better approximations and implementations in programming settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Approximation Fixpoint Theory (AFT) methods for non-monotone reasoning are coarse and computationally expensive, which limits their practical usefulness in programming languages.

Method: The paper introduces an abstract interpretation to improve efficiency and controlled unsoundness to refine approximations. The proposed algorithm ensures soundness, is anytime, guarantees termination on finite-height lattices, and can be modified for polynomial-time complexity.

Result: The methods are instantiated in answer set programming and speculative program analysis, proving practical applicability by accelerating preprocessing for convergence and reducing rollback, all while maintaining soundness.

Conclusion: The research enhances AFT's practicality by addressing its inefficiency and coarse approximations, offering a sound and efficient solution for non-monotone processes in PL settings.

Abstract: Many modern solvers and program analyzers rely on non-monotone reasoning (e.g. negation-as-failure, speculative updates, backtracking) for which classical monotone fixed-point methods do not apply. The general problem of finding the fixed points of these processes is a difficult one. For this reason, there have been theoretical efforts in existing Approximation Fixpoint Theory (AFT) from the domain of logic programming to approximate fixed points of non-monotone operators. Tight approximations of these fixed points are highly useful for accelerating non-monotonic computations by restricting the search space. In practice, however, even the best approximations obtained through AFT can be coarse and computationally expensive. We aim to address both issues to make AFT approximation methods practical for use in programming languages (PL) settings. To mitigate inefficiency, we prove the soundness of an abstract interpretation for approximating operators. To improve upon coarse approximations, we carefully introduce controlled unsoundness to design an effective yet practical algorithm for partitioning and tightening AFT's best approximations. This algorithm is sound, anytime, and guarantees termination on finite-height lattices. We further present a modification that ensures polynomial-time complexity. We instantiate these methods in two settings: (1) answer set programming, where it serves as a convergence-accelerating pre-processor, and (2) speculative program analysis, where it reduces rollback while preserving soundness. In both settings, we focus on implementation-level details to demonstrate the practical applicability of our methods.

</details>


### [610] [A New Interaction Concept for Interactive and Autoactive Program Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06972)
*Wolfram Pfeifer,Mattias Ulbrich,Daniel Drodt*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper introduces an interactive approach to program verification that minimizes the gap between source code and logical encoding, implemented as a plugin for the Java verification engine KeY.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current program verifiers have limited interactivity and often force users to operate at a lower level or rely heavily on source code annotations, which complicates understanding and debugging of proof states.

Method: The authors propose a novel interaction concept where users can inspect and interact with the proof state directly on source code and specification levels, implemented as a plugin.

Result: A user study demonstrated that the prototype helps users understand proof states better and locate issues in source code or specifications.

Conclusion: The presented approach makes program verification more intuitive and improves user capability to identify defects, thus addressing key limitations of existing tools.

Abstract: Fully functional program verification is an undecidable$\unicode{x2014}$and, hence, inherently difficult$\unicode{x2014}$task, that is not automatically solvable but typically requires user interaction and guidance. Existing verifiers either work autoactively, requiring the user to write annotations in source code, without the possibility to inspect the proof state or intervene in case of an unsuccessful attempt, or allow interactions on a logical encoding that is on a lower level than the user-provided specifications. We present a novel interaction concept which allows the user to inspect and interact with the proof state on source code and specification level. This minimizes the mental gap between the representations. We provide an implementation of the concept as a plugin for the Java verification engine KeY, and show with a user study that this prototype can be beneficial for users to understand the proof state and find defects in source code or specifications.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [611] [Modular Lie Algebraic PDE Control of Multibody Flexible Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06709)
*Sadeq Yaqubi,Jouni Mattila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a control framework for flexible robotic systems using screw-theoretic Lie-algebraic modeling and PDEs, ensuring exact stability and tracking error convergence in multibody manipulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a robust and scalable control scheme for serial manipulators in 3D space while addressing challenges such as deformations and dynamic interactions.

Method: The method involves utilizing screw-theoretic Lie-algebraic models, substituting strain-based PDEs, implementing deflection-compensating inverse kinematics, and ensuring stability via Lyapunov and energy arguments.

Result: The framework ensures global exponential convergence of tracking errors, eliminates inter-link dynamic power terms, and guarantees bounded deformation for chains of arbitrary length.

Conclusion: The proposed modular approach offers exact interaction cancellation and stability in multibody robotic systems, validated by numerical simulations highlighting physical consistency.

Abstract: This paper addresses PDE-based control for flexible multibody robotic systems, presenting a subsystem-based framework for serial manipulators with arbitrary links in 3D space. The approach uses a screw-theoretic Lie-algebraic model where motion, deformation, and forces are expressed as body-fixed twists and wrenches in se(3). By substituting a strain-based deformation PDE into the dynamics, distributed elastic acceleration is eliminated, yielding a model governed by twist acceleration and the deformation field. Subsystem twist trajectories are generated from task-space endpoints via deflection-compensating inverse kinematics, providing real-time correction for tip deformation. A nominal controller for each link ensures exponential decay of twist errors via a Lyapunov function nu_i. An adaptive modification replaces physical parameters with online estimates, establishing exponential convergence of both twist and parameter errors. Summing over all links, composite Lyapunov functions V = sum(nu_i) and V^a = sum(nu_i^a) yield time derivatives where inter-link interaction power terms telescope to zero. This cancellation is ensured by Newton's third law and the frame invariance of the power pairing on se(3) x se*(3), establishing global exponential convergence of tracking errors. Bounded elastic deformation is guaranteed by an Euler-Bernoulli energy argument. The screw-theoretic structure renders interaction cancellation exact, making the stability certificate modular and scalable to chains of arbitrary length. Numerical simulations demonstrate the scheme's physical consistency.

</details>


### [612] [An Aerial Manipulator for Perception-Driven Flower Targeting Toward Contactless Pollination in Vertical Farming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06759)
*Chenzhe Jin,Zhuohang Wu,Yifan Cai,Xiangqi Li,Jan Ming Kevin Tan,Narsimlu Kemsaram,Valerio Modugno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a robotic aerial manipulator platform designed for flower detection, localization, and precise positioning in vertical farming environments, addressing the lack of natural pollinators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work is motivated by the decline of natural pollinators and the challenges of crop production in controlled environments like vertical farming, where natural insect pollination is absent.

Method: The researchers developed a system combining RGBD-based perception, model predictive path integral (MPPI) control for UAVs on a PX4 platform, and a 2DoF manipulator for precise positioning. The platform was tested in both simulations and lab experiments.

Result: The system demonstrated stable UAV flight, reliable flower localization, and centimeter-level positioning in experiments, along with consistent trajectory convergence and accurate alignment in simulations.

Conclusion: The study validates the aerial manipulator as a dependable robotic framework for precise flower targeting in vertical farming, setting the stage for future integration of advanced contactless pollination techniques.

Abstract: The decline of natural pollinators has created a major challenge for crop production in controlled indoor agriculture, particularly in vertical farming environments where natural insect pollination is absent. This motivates the development of robotic systems capable of performing precise flower targeting tasks while minimizing physical interference with delicate floral structures. This paper presents an aerial manipulator platform for perception driven flower detection, localization, and approach in vertical farming environments. The proposed system integrates onboard RGBD based perception, model predictive path integral (MPPI) based unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) control on a PX4 platform, and a lightweight 2DoF manipulator for precise end effector positioning. The platform is evaluated in both MuJoCo simulation and UAV lab experiments using a flower targeting testbed. The experimental results demonstrate stable UAV flight, reliable flower localization, and centimeter level end effector positioning accuracy. In simulation, the proposed controller achieves consistent trajectory convergence and accurate target alignment. In the real world UAV lab environment, the integrated perception control manipulation framework enables stable flower targeted positioning and end effector alignment under constrained aerial operation. These results validate the proposed aerial manipulator as a robust robotic carrier and positioning framework for future contactless pollination systems. While the current study focuses on perception guided targeting and positioning, the developed platform provides a practical foundation for integrating advanced contactless end effectors, including acoustic based pollen manipulation modules, in future work.

</details>


### [613] [Bi3: A Biplatform, Bicultural, Biperson Dataset for Social Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06863)
*Andrew Stratton,Phani Teja Singamaneni,Pranav Goyal,Rachid Alami,Christoforos Mavrogiannis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The Bi3 dataset presents unique and diverse multimodal data for understanding human-robot interaction during navigation in constrained spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance social robot navigation by collecting diverse, real-world data on human-robot interaction in constrained environments.

Method: Recorded robot-human interactions using five navigation algorithms, two robot platforms, and data from 74 participants across two sites in the USA and France.

Result: The dataset includes 10.5 hours of motion tracks, videos, and user feedback, providing a benchmark for human motion prediction and robot navigation modeling.

Conclusion: Bi3 contributes to improving robot navigation and supports research in human-robot interaction in complex, crowded spaces.

Abstract: We contribute Bi3, a dataset of social robot navigation among groups of people in a constrained lab space. Compared to prior data collection efforts for social robot navigation, our dataset is unique in that it features: an original experiment design giving rise to close navigation encounters between two humans and a robot; five different navigation algorithms; two different robot platforms; a diverse participant pool of 74 people recruited from two sites in the USA and France; multimodal data streams including 10.5 hours of human and robot ground-truth motion tracks, RGB video, and user impressions over robot performance. Our analysis of the collected dataset through metrics like interaction density and human velocity suggests that Bi3 represents a benchmark of unique diversity and modeling complexity. Bi3 contributes towards understanding how humans and robots can productively mesh their activities in constrained environments, and can be a resource for training models of human motion prediction and robot control policies for navigation in densely crowded spaces.

</details>


### [614] [Traffic Scenario Orchestration from Language via Constraint Satisfaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06966)
*Frieda Rong,Chris Zhang,Kelvin Wong,Raquel Urtasun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The authors propose using a constraint-solving approach combined with foundation model reasoning to automate driving scenario generation for AVs, outperforming other methods in generating scenarios via natural language descriptions and capturing ego-reactive behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Testing scenarios for autonomous vehicles often require specific interactions with the AV's policy and typically lie outside standard distributions, making manual creation labor-intensive and statistical modeling ineffective.

Method: The study integrates natural language-based descriptions interpreted by foundation models into a constraint-solving framework to orchestrate a range of driving scenarios for AV testing.

Result: Results demonstrate that the proposed methodology achieves high success rates in orchestrating diverse scenarios compared to baseline methods, especially for ego-reactive specifications.

Conclusion: This paper presents a scalable solution for generating out-of-distribution driving scenarios by employing a synthesis of constraint solving and AI models, enabling efficient and precise AV testing.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) require extensive testing in simulation, but test case generation for driving scenarios is laborious. The desired scenarios are often out-of-distribution and have precise requirements on interactions with the AV policy under test. Manually programming scenarios allows for precise controllability but is difficult to scale. On the other hand, statistical models can leverage compute and data, but struggle with precise controllability when out-of-distribution. We cast scenario orchestration as a constraint-solving problem and present a language-in, simulation-out scenario orchestrator for closed-loop testing AVs. Our approach leverages foundation model reasoning to translate general, natural language descriptions into a set of constraints as a scenario representation. This then allows us to leverage off the shelf solvers to solve for actor behaviors which meet precise testing intentions in closed-loop. Under a benchmark of carefully crafted and diverse scenario descriptions, our approach greatly outperforms our baselines in orchestration success rate. We further show that our closed-loop approach is especially important for scenarios which require ego-reactive specifications.

</details>


### [615] [AirBender: Adaptive Transportation of Bendable Objects Using Dual UAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07003)
*Jiawei Xu,Longsen Gao,Rafael Fierro,David Saldaña*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes an adaptive controller for two aerial robots to collaboratively transport bendable objects without relying on explicit elasticity models, ensuring stability and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Handling bendable objects midair is challenging, especially for aerial robots, due to limited actuation and stability issues.

Method: Developed an adaptive controller allowing on-the-fly adjustment to unknown deformable properties of objects. Lyapunov analysis ensures stability, and hardware experiments validate the approach.

Result: The method proves to be stable and effective, with successful trajectory tracking in various scenarios using multiple aerial vehicles.

Conclusion: The research enables collaborative aerial robots to safely and effectively transport bendable objects without predefined elasticity models.

Abstract: The interaction of robots with bendable objects in midair presents significant challenges in control, often resulting in performance degradation and potential crashes, especially for aerial robots due to their limited actuation capabilities and constant need to remain airborne. This paper presents an adaptive controller that enables two aerial vehicles to collaboratively follow a trajectory while transporting a bendable object without relying on explicit elasticity models. Our method allows on-the-fly adaptation to the object's unknown deformable properties, ensuring stability and performance in trajectory-tracking tasks. We use Lyapunov analysis to demonstrate that our adaptive controller is asymptotically stable. Our method is evaluated through hardware experiments in various scenarios, demonstrating the capabilities of using multirotor aerial vehicles to handle bendable objects.

</details>


### [616] [Intention assimilation control for accurate tracking with variable impedance in teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07037)
*Atsushi Takagi,Yanan Li,Hiroaki Gomi,Etienne Burdet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The proposed Intention Assimilation Control (IAC) improves tracking accuracy while allowing on-the-fly adjustment of follower robot impedance, addressing the trade-off between safety and precision in teleoperation systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the trade-off in teleoperation systems where high stiffness improves tracking accuracy but compromises safety, and low stiffness is safer but reduces accuracy.

Method: IAC estimates and transmits the leader's target position to the follower instead of the leader's current position. This allows for dynamic impedance adjustment either by user intention or task requirements.
