Specifically, we show that both a theta-timescale (4-8 Hz) inhibitory current $I_m$ and a slower delta-timescale (1-4 Hz) inhibitory potassium current $I_{\rm K_{SS}}$ are crucial for entrainment flexibility. Their interaction creates a three-timescale structure that gives rise to pronounced delay phenomena associated with a delayed Hopf bifurcation (DHB). Interestingly, the superslow $I_{\rm K_{SS}}$ and the associated DHB play little role in the unforced oscillatory dynamics, but are recruited to support phase locking under external forcing. Moreover, the intermediate-timescale current $I_m$, rather than being redundant, further expands the phase-locking range by prolonging delayed recovery along the superslow manifold. Together, these results suggest that coordination among intrinsic inhibitory currents operating on multiple timescales may represent a key mechanism supporting flexible phase locking to rhythmic inputs in the brain.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [675] [How Does Attention Help? Insights from Random Matrices on Signal Recovery from Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06826)
*Mohamed El Amine Seddik*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper studies high-dimensional sample covariance matrices from pooled sequence representations with fixed attention weights, deriving limits on spectral properties and optimal signal recovery conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve signal recovery from sequence representations pooled via fixed attention mechanisms, especially in high-dimensional settings.

Method: Analyzing spectral properties of covariance matrices using mathematical tools like non-Marchenko–Pastur laws and free multiplicative convolution in high-dimensional regimes.

Result: Exact characterizations of eigenvalue distributions, phase transitions in signal recovery, and optimal attention weights are derived; simulations validate the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Optimal attention weights enhance signal recovery, and causal self-attention with appropriate scaling improves recovery under specific signal distribution conditions.

Abstract: We study the spectral properties of sample covariance matrices constructed from pooled sequence representations, where token embeddings are drawn from a fixed two-class Gaussian mixture table and pooled via (fixed) attention weights. Working in the high-dimensional regime $d,V,N\to\infty$ with $d/V\toδ$ and $d/N\toγ$, we derive exact characterizations of the limiting eigenvalue distribution, outlier eigenvalues, and eigenvector alignment with the hidden signal. The bulk spectrum follows a non-Marchenko--Pastur law given by the free multiplicative convolution $κ(MP_δ\boxtimes MP_γ)$, reflecting the finite vocabulary structure. Signal recovery undergoes two successive BBP-type phase transitions characterized by the scalars: $δ,γ,α=w^{\top} R w$ and $κ=\|w\|^2$, where $w$ denotes the attention pooling weights and $R$ the positional correlation matrix. An aftermath of our analysis demonstrates that the optimal attention weights maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio $α/κ$ are given by the (normalized) top eigenvector of $R$, and we show (as a particular case of our analysis) that parameter-free causal self-attention with $τ/d$ score scaling yields deterministic harmonic weights that improve signal recovery over mean pooling whenever early tokens carry more signal. Extensive simulations confirm sharp agreement between theory and finite-dimensional experiments.

</details>


### [676] [One Operator for Many Densities: Amortized Approximation of Conditioning by Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06873)
*Panos Tsimpos,Edoardo Calvello,Ayoub Belhadji,Nicholas H. Nelsen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new approach to probabilistic conditioning by proposing a general operator that maps any joint density to its conditional, leveraging neural operators to achieve accurate results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Probabilistic conditioning is a key aspect of scientific and engineering tasks that require modeling uncertainty, but traditional methods focus on learning conditional distributions for fixed joint distributions. The authors aim to generalize and simplify this process.

Method: The authors propose identifying and learning a universal conditioning operator, which can map any joint density to the corresponding conditional distribution. They utilize neural operators and establish theoretical continuity guarantees.

Result: The paper theoretically proves that the conditioning operator can be approximated to an arbitrary degree of accuracy by neural operators and empirically demonstrates its applicability for Gaussian mixtures.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a pathway for general-purpose, amortized approaches to probabilistic conditioning and showcases potential applications for foundational Bayesian inference models.

Abstract: Probabilistic conditioning is concerned with the identification of a distribution of a random variable $X$ given a random variable $Y$. It is a cornerstone of scientific and engineering applications where modeling uncertainty is key. This problem has traditionally been addressed in machine learning by directly learning the conditional distribution of a fixed joint distribution. This paper introduces a novel perspective: we propose to solve the conditioning problem by identifying a single operator that maps any joint density to its conditional, thus amortizing over joint-conditional pairs. We establish that the conditioning operator can be approximated to arbitrary accuracy by neural operators. Our proof relies on new results establishing continuity of the conditioning operator over suitable classes of densities. Finally, we learn the conditioning map for a class of Gaussian mixtures using neural operators, illustrating the promise of our framework. This work provides the theoretical underpinnings for general-purpose, amortized methods for probabilistic conditioning, such as foundation models for Bayesian inference.

</details>


### [677] [Every Feedforward Neural Network Definable in an o-Minimal Structure Has Finite Sample Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07097)
*Anastasis Kratsios,Gregory Cousins,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde,Bum Jun Kim,Simone Brugiapaglia*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that feedforward neural network architectures, defined within o-minimal structures, have finite sample complexity under the PAC learning model, covering a wide range of modern architectures and operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish finite-sample PAC learnability as a general property of standard feedforward neural networks and highlight its implications for architectural design and machine learning research.

Method: The authors utilize the theoretical framework of o-minimal structures to characterize feedforward architectures and prove their finite sample complexity in the agnostic PAC learning setting.

Result: All fixed finite feedforward architectures, including MLPs, CNNs, GNNs, and transformers, are shown to have finite sample complexity due to their tame computational properties, regardless of specific activations or VC arguments.

Conclusion: Finite-sample PAC learnability is a common feature of feedforward architectures, emphasizing the importance of inductive biases, geometric properties, and optimization mechanisms in design decisions.

Abstract: We show that, in a precise sense, a broad class of feedforward neural networks learn (have finite sample complexity) in the PAC model: every fixed finite feedforward architecture whose layers are definable in an o-minimal structure has finite sample complexity in the agnostic PAC setting, even with unbounded parameters. This covers standard fixed-size MLPs, CNNs, GNNs, and transformers with fixed sequence length, together with the operations and layers typically used in such architectures, including linear projections, residual connections, attention mechanisms, pooling layers, normalization layers, and admissible positional encodings. Hence, distribution-free learnability for modern non-recurrent architectures is not an exceptional property of particular activations or architecture-specific VC arguments, but a consequence of tame feedforward computation. Our results reposition finite-sample PAC learnability as a baseline rather than a differentiator: they shift the focus of architectural comparison toward inductive biases, symmetries and geometric priors, scalability, and optimization behaviour.

</details>


### [678] [Kernel Selection is Model Selection: A Unified Complexity-Penalized Approach for MMD Two-Sample Tests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06883)
*Yijin Ni,Xiaoming Huo*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces Complexity-Penalized MMD (CP-MMD), an approach to solve kernel selection challenges in nonparametric two-sample testing by bounding kernel complexity, enabling robust and scalable optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MMD-based two-sample testing depends heavily on kernel choice, but fixed kernels cannot distinguish certain distributions and traditional optimization methods either overfit or fail to scale effectively to continuous search spaces.

Method: The authors propose CP-MMD, which applies a complexity penalty derived from two-sample concentration inequalities to account for kernel search complexity, enabling data-driven optimization over continuous kernel spaces like deep learning models.

Result: CP-MMD maximizes true test power across various kernel parameterizations (e.g., linear, polynomial, deep learning) and ensures Type-I error validity, outperforming existing methods in power while enabling grid-free optimization.

Conclusion: CP-MMD successfully resolves the dichotomy in kernel optimization by ensuring robust test performance with scalable kernel selection, significantly advancing the MMD-based two-sample testing framework.

Abstract: The Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) is a cornerstone statistic for nonparametric two-sample testing, but its test power is dictated entirely by the chosen kernel. Because any fixed kernel inherently fails to distinguish certain distributions, the kernel must be dynamically optimized. However, data-driven optimization violates the foundational i.i.d. assumption, forcing a strict trade-off in existing frameworks. Ratio criteria ignore this dependence, inducing overfitting and variance collapse on rich kernel classes. Conversely, aggregation methods bypass the dependence using finite grids, but this strategy cannot scale to continuous search spaces like deep kernels.
  To break this dichotomy, we establish data-driven kernel selection as a model selection problem. We propose Complexity-Penalized MMD (CP-MMD), a criterion derived by applying the two-sample uniform concentration inequality of preceding works to the post-optimization MMD problem. The resulting penalty bounds the empirical MMD by the complexity of the kernel search space, mathematically absorbing the cost of optimization, so that CP-MMD enables direct, grid-free maximization over continuous parametric classes, including scalar bandwidths, polynomial feature bandwidths, and deep network parameters. By formally accounting for optimization complexity, we prove that CP-MMD maximizes true test power while ensuring unconditional Type-I validity. Consequently, CP-MMD enables grid-free kernel selection across linear, polynomial-feature, and deep regimes, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art test power.

</details>


### [679] [Locally Near Optimal Piecewise Linear Regression in High Dimensions via Difference of Max-Affine Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06959)
*Haitham Kanj,Kiryung Lee*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces an Adaptive Block Gradient Descent (ABGD) algorithm for solving piecewise linear regression efficiently, backed by theoretical guarantees and competitive real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to provide a robust parametrized solution for piecewise linear regression problems, exploiting the formulation of these functions as differences of max-affine functions.

Method: The method involves the development of ABGD, a gradient descent algorithm initialized using a prior method for max-affine functions. It operates effectively under sub-Gaussian covariate and noise distributions, with linear convergence rates.

Result: ABGD converges linearly to highly accurate estimates with near-minimax optimal sample complexity. Synthetic and real-world performance evaluations show strong results against competing approaches.

Conclusion: ABGD provides a theoretically sound and practically effective solution for piecewise linear regression, achieving impressive accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: This paper presents a parametric solution to piecewise linear regression through the Adaptive Block Gradient Descent (ABGD) algorithm. The heart of the method is the parametrization of piecewise linear functions as the difference of max-affine (DoMA) functions. A non-asymptotic local convergence analysis for ABGD is provided under sub-Gaussian covariate and noise distributions. To initialize ABGD, we adapt a prior algorithm originally developed for the simpler setting of max-affine functions. When suitably initialized, ABGD converges linearly to an $ε$-accurate estimate given $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d\max(σ_z/ε,1)^2)$ observations where $σ_z^2$ denotes the noise variance. This implies exact recovery given $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ samples in the noiseless case. Also, such a rate is shown to be minimax optimal up to logarithmic factors. Synthetic numerical results corroborate the theoretical guarantees for ABGD. We also observe competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art methods on real-world datasets.

</details>


### [680] [A Differentiable Bayesian Relaxation for Latent Partial-Order Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06976)
*Dongqing Li,Geoff K. Nicholls,Shiyi Sun,You Luo*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a differentiable approach to infer latent partial orders from noisy linear-order data, improving computational efficiency while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the issue of improperly recorded datasets as linear orders, while their actual structure is only partially ordered. This is common in agent and workflow traces.

Method: The authors propose a differentiable relaxation model that smoothens the inference for latent partial orders. They substitute rigid constraints with smooth surrogates, allowing gradient-based MCMC and variational inference.

Result: The method demonstrates fidelity to the original model for small datasets and offers a better trade-off between runtime and accuracy for larger datasets when tested on synthetic, social dominance relation, and cloud-agent trace data.

Conclusion: This approach creates a novel framework for partial-order inference with significant improvements in runtime efficiency and accurate posterior estimations.

Abstract: Many ranking and agent trace datasets are recorded as linear orders even though their latent structure is only partially ordered. This is especially common in agent and workflow traces, where observed order may reflect arbitrary linearization rather than true prerequisites. We introduce a differentiable relaxation for latent partial-order inference from such traces. Starting from a hard frontier-constrained model of noisy linear extensions, we replace discontinuous product-order precedence and binary frontier feasibility with smooth surrogates, yielding a continuous posterior that preserves closure-level partial-order semantics and supports gradient-based MCMC and variational inference. We prove soft transitivity, sharp-limit frontier recovery, and convergence to the hard likelihood. Experiments on synthetic data, records of social dominance relations, and cloud-agent traces show close posterior fidelity to hard MCMC on small instances and improved runtime--accuracy trade-offs on larger problems.

</details>


### [681] [BGM-IV: an AI-powered Bayesian generative modeling approach for instrumental variable analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07029)
*Guyue Luo,Qiao Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents BGM-IV, a latent Bayesian generative modeling method for nonlinear instrumental-variable (IV) regression, designed to handle high-dimensional covariates and endogeneity. It demonstrates competitive performance on low-dimensional datasets and outperforms in high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern IV regression often falters when addressing nonlinear effects and high-dimensional covariates. Current methods face challenges in capturing causal information embedded in high-dimensional spaces.

Method: BGM-IV employs a latent Bayesian generative model that structures the causal problem in a latent space. It distinguishes shared confounding, outcome-specific variations, and treatment-specific variations while using IV-integrated pseudo-likelihood to address endogeneity.

Result: BGM-IV outperforms or matches existing methods in various benchmark datasets, particularly excelling in high-dimensional covariate settings.

Conclusion: Structured latent generative modeling, as implemented in BGM-IV, offers an effective solution for nonlinear IV estimation in scenarios involving rich covariates.

Abstract: Instrumental-variable (IV) regression enables causal estimation under endogeneity, but modern IV problems often involve nonlinear structural effects and high-dimensional covariates. Existing nonlinear IV methods directly learn the causal relation in observed feature space or rely on learned representations within two-stage or moment-based procedures, which can struggle when the causal information is embedded in a high-dimensional representation. We propose BGM-IV, a latent Bayesian generative modeling approach that reframes nonlinear IV regression as posterior inference in a causally structured latent space. BGM-IV infers latent components that separately capture shared confounding structure, outcome-specific variation, treatment-specific variation, and covariate-only nuisance information. To account for endogeneity, BGM-IV replaces the confounded outcome likelihood with an IV-integrated pseudo-likelihood that averages over instrument-induced treatment values within the latent model. Across various benchmark datasets, BGM-IV remains competitive in the classical low-dimensional regime and performs best in high-dimensional covariate regimes. Together, these results show that structured latent generative modeling provides a principled and effective strategy to nonlinear IV estimation with rich covariates. The code of BGM-IV is available at https://github.com/liuq-lab/BGM-IV.

</details>


### [682] [An Interpretable and Scalable Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07046)
*Xinhao Qu,Qiang Heng,Hao Zeng,Xiaoqian Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new interpretable and scalable method for evaluating large language models using a reformulated Item Response Theory framework, achieving speed and accuracy improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for LLMs focus on average accuracy, neglecting output variability and benchmark item differences. While Item Response Theory offers a structured solution, it's computationally impractical for large-scale use.

Method: The authors propose a method based on the majorization-minimization principle, reformulating the evaluation process into constrained matrix factorization subproblems for efficient and stable parameter estimation.

Result: Experiments using synthetic and real-world datasets show the proposed method outperforms others in scalability, interpretability, and speed, with comparable or better accuracy.

Conclusion: The method aligns with established scaling laws, provides insights into item characteristics, and supports more principled and efficient benchmark designs for LLM evaluation.

Abstract: Evaluation of large language models (LLMs) is increasingly critical, yet standard benchmarking methods rely on average accuracy, overlooking both the inherent stochasticity of LLM outputs and the heterogeneity of benchmark items. Item Response Theory (IRT) offers a principled framework for modeling latent model abilities and item characteristics, but conventional methods are computationally expensive and numerically unstable, limiting large-scale implementations. To address these challenges, we propose an interpretable and scalable framework for LLM evaluation based on the majorization-minimization principle. Our approach reformulates the problem as a sequence of constrained matrix factorization subproblems, enabling stable and efficient parameter estimation with theoretical guarantees for identifiability and convergence. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including MATH-500 and six Open LLM Leaderboard benchmarks, demonstrate that our method achieves superior scalability and interpretability. It delivers orders-of-magnitude speedups over competing methods while maintaining comparable or even higher estimation accuracy. Our results align with established scaling laws and offer insights into item difficulty and discrimination, informing more principled benchmark design.

</details>


### [683] [Causal EpiNets: Precision-corrected Bounds on Individual Treatment Effects using Epistemic Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07065)
*Gandharv Patil,Keyi Tang,Raquel Aoki,Leo Guelman*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper addresses issues with standard plug-in estimators for finite-sample PNS and introduces a neural framework that ensures structural constraint satisfaction and corrects extremum bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the limitations of standard plug-in estimators for PNS estimation, which fail to meet structural probability constraints and introduce extremum bias in finite samples.

Method: The authors propose an anchored neural architecture to ensure structural constraint satisfaction and utilize precision-corrected inference via Epistemic Neural Networks for high-dimensional uncertainty quantification.

Result: The method achieves nominal coverage and ensures structural constraint validity in scenarios where standard estimators underperform, especially in high-dimensional settings.

Conclusion: The proposed neural framework provides a robust and scalable solution for accurate finite-sample PNS estimation, addressing critical shortcomings of traditional methods.

Abstract: Individual treatment effects are not point-identified from data. The Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) circumvents this limitation by characterizing individual-level causality through intersection bounds derived from combined experimental and observational data. In finite samples, however, standard plug-in estimators systematically fail: they violate structural probability constraints and suffer from extremum bias induced by max-min operators, yielding spuriously narrow intervals. We propose a neural framework for finite-sample PNS estimation that resolves both pathologies. We introduce an anchored neural architecture that guarantees structural constraint satisfaction by construction. To correct extremum bias, we employ precision-corrected intersection-bound inference, leveraging Epistemic Neural Networks for scalable, high-dimensional uncertainty quantification. Empirical evaluations confirm that this approach maintains nominal coverage and exact constraint validity in high-dimensional regimes where standard estimators systematically undercover.

</details>


### [684] [TRACE: Transport Alignment Conformal Prediction via Diffusion and Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07100)
*Zhenhan Fang,Aixin Tan,Jian Huang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces TRACE, a novel framework for conformal prediction in multi-dimensional outputs using transport alignment. It addresses limitations of existing methods by avoiding restrictive assumptions and explicit likelihood evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for a conformal prediction method that works for complex generative settings, avoiding limitations like restrictive geometric assumptions and likelihood evaluation.

Method: TRACE uses stochastic transport trajectories in diffusion and flow models to define nonconformity based on transport alignment, calibrating the scores with split conformal prediction.

Result: TRACE offers valid coverage, adapts to complex distributions (e.g., multimodal, non-convex), and demonstrates robustness in synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: TRACE provides a distribution-free, valid, and flexible approach for conformal predictions in multi-dimensional outputs, addressing existing methodological gaps.

Abstract: Constructing valid and informative conformal prediction regions for multi-dimensional outputs remains a fundamental challenge. While conformal prediction provides finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantees, its practical performance critically depends on the choice of nonconformity score. Existing approaches often rely on restrictive geometric assumptions or require explicit likelihood evaluation and invertible transformations, limiting their applicability in complex generative settings.
  In this work, we introduce TRACE (TRansport Alignment Conformal Estimation), a conformal prediction framework that defines nonconformity through transport alignment in diffusion and flow matching models. Rather than evaluating likelihoods, we measure how well a candidate output aligns with the learned generative dynamics by averaging denoising or velocity-matching errors along stochastic transport trajectories. The resulting transport-based scores are scalar-valued and can be calibrated using split conformal prediction, yielding valid marginal coverage under exchangeability. We further analyze the statistical properties of the proposed scores and their sensitivity to computational budget. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate valid coverage and show that the resulting regions adapt naturally to multimodal and non-convex conditional distributions.

</details>


### [685] [Classification Fields: Arbitrarily Fine Recursive Hierarchical Clustering From Few Examples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07119)
*Yicen Li,Ruiyang Hong,Anastasis Kratsios,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde,Paul D. McNicholas*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces classification fields for infinite-depth hierarchical clustering using a local refinement rule, ensuring scalable and accurate clustering predictions beyond observed data. Experimental validation supports its practicality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical clustering methods fail to capture infinite-depth hierarchical structures with recursive geometric refinement, necessitating a method capable of extending clustering insight beyond finite data.

Method: A classification field generator recursively creates hierarchical cluster centers and metrics using a refinement rule. A predictor approximates this generator from finite data, with provable accuracy guarantees and experimental validation on simulated and real-world hierarchies.

Result: The method achieves provable exponential truncation convergence and efficient network realizations, preserving ordered child slots, geometry, and hierarchy metrics under recursive rollout.

Conclusion: Finite hierarchical observations can be leveraged to learn local refinement rules for generating deeper classification fields, achieving scalable and accurate clustering predictions.

Abstract: Classical clustering methods usually return either a finite partition of the observed data or a finite dendrogram over it. This finite-sample view is inadequate when the hierarchy of interest is a recursive geometric object with fine-scale refinements that continue beyond the levels directly observed. We introduce classification fields: infinite-depth hierarchical cluster structures on $\mathbb{R}^d$ generated by a local parent-to-child refinement rule. A classification field generator maps each parent centre to an ordered, bounded, and separated tuple of child residuals. Together with a root and a scale factor, this rule recursively generates cluster centres, Voronoi cells, and a metric DAG encoding the hierarchy. Given only a finite prefix of such a hierarchy, we learn a classification field predictor that approximates the generator and can be rolled out to unseen depths. We prove exponential truncation convergence in the completed cell metric and ReLU realizability with width $O(\varepsilon^{-γ})$ and depth $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3γ/2})$, where $γ=\log K/(-\log s)$, up to finite-window aspect-ratio factors. The approximation holds at the level of the induced compact metric structures, measured in the completed cell-metric Hausdorff distance. Experimental validation on matched CFG-generated hierarchies, IFS fractals, and image-induced recursive clustering hierarchies shows that learned predictors preserve ordered child slots, unordered geometry, and hierarchy-level path metrics under recursive rollout. These results support the claim that finite hierarchical observations can reveal local refinement rules capable of generating substantially deeper classification fields.

</details>


### [686] [Spectrum-Adaptive Generalization Bounds for Trained Deep Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07297)
*Mana Sakai,Masaaki Imaizumi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper derives spectrum-adaptive generalization bounds for Transformers that offer a deeper exploration of how their spectral structure influences generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why Transformers generalize well and to address limitations of existing norm-based generalization bounds, which suffer from fixed constraints and unfavorable scaling with depth.

Method: The paper develops spectrum-adaptive post hoc generalization bounds using layerwise spectral norm control and Schatten matrix quantities. The approach adapts spectral complexity trade-offs based on learned singular-value profiles after training.

Result: The derived bounds grow more slowly with depth and hidden dimension than existing norm-based bounds, as shown through empirical evaluation of BERT-adapted proxies.

Conclusion: The results introduce a new complexity-based perspective to analyze Transformers’ generalization by highlighting the role of spectral structure and adapting to learned characteristics.

Abstract: Understanding why trained Transformers generalize well is a fundamental problem in modern machine learning theory, and complexity-based generalization bounds provide a principled way to study this question. While existing norm-based bounds for Transformers remove the explicit polynomial dependence on the hidden dimension, they typically impose fixed norm constraints specified a priori and can exhibit unfavorable exponential dependence on depth. In this paper, we derive spectrum-adaptive post hoc generalization bounds for multi-layer Transformers. Under layerwise spectral norm control, the bounds are expressed in terms of layerwise Schatten quantities of the query-key, value, and feedforward weight matrices. Since the Schatten indices need not be fixed a priori and can instead be selected after training, separately for each matrix type and layer, the bounds adaptively trade off spectral complexity against the dimension- and depth-dependent factors according to the learned singular-value profiles. Empirical comparisons of BERT-adapted proxies for the leading complexity factors suggest that the proxies induced by our bounds grow more slowly with depth and hidden dimension than the corresponding norm-based proxies. Overall, our results provide a complexity-based perspective on how the spectral structure of trained Transformers is reflected in generalization analyses.

</details>


### [687] [A Refined Generalization Analysis for Extreme Multi-class Supervised Contrastive Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07596)
*Nong Minh Hieu,Antoine Ledent*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper focuses on improving the theoretical sample complexity of Contrastive Representation Learning (CRL), especially in scenarios with dependent contrastive tuples and long-tailed class distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of theoretical understanding in CRL's sample complexity, particularly under practical conditions where contrastive tuples are dependent due to being drawn from a finite labeled pool.

Method: The authors improve upon recent U-Statistics-based methods, providing a class-independent sample complexity bound and introducing a new estimator that captures risk concentration across classes for sharper bounds.

Result: The paper achieves bounds that scale with the number of classes $R$ instead of the rarest class probability $ρ_{\min}$ and shows a sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}(k)$ under certain assumptions, suitable for extreme multi-class and long-tailed distributions.

Conclusion: The proposed methods offer more practical and less pessimistic theoretical sample complexity bounds for CRL, which are particularly applicable in challenging scenarios like extreme multi-class and long-tailed learning.

Abstract: Contrastive Representation Learning (CRL) has achieved strong empirical success in multiple machine learning disciplines, yet its theoretical sample complexity remains poorly understood. Existing analyses usually assume that input tuples are identically and independently distributed, an assumption violated in most practical settings where contrastive tuples are constructed from a finite pool of labeled data, inducing dependencies among tuples. While one recent work analyzed this learning setting using U-Statistics to estimate the population risk, the techniques used therein require the risk of each class to concentrate uniformly, making excess risk bounds scale in the order of $ρ_{\min}^{-{1}/{2}}$ where $ρ_{\min}$ denotes the probability of the rarest class. Such a dependency can be overly pessimistic in the extreme multiclass settings where there are many tail classes which contribute minimally to the overall population risk. Our contributions are two-fold. Firstly, we improve upon the previous work and prove a bound with a sample complexity of the same order as the number of classes $R$, regardless of the distribution over classes. Furthermore, we formulate a different estimator that captures the concentration of the risk \textit{across classes}, enabling sharper bounds in extreme multi-class learning scenarios, especially where class distributions are long-tailed. Under mild assumptions on the class distributions, the resulting sample complexity is $\mathcal{O}(k)$ where $k$ is the number of samples per tuple.

</details>


### [688] [Reliable Chain-of-Thought via Prefix Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07654)
*Naoto Iwase,Yuki Ichihara,Mohammad Atif Quamar,Junpei Komiyama*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes utilizing a novel 'prefix consistency' metric for improving reasoning accuracy in large language models, reducing computational costs while maintaining or improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiencies in majority voting (MV)-based reasoning accuracy enhancement methods, which require multiple regenerations during test time, resulting in high computational costs.

Method: The authors introduce 'prefix consistency,' a reliability metric that evaluates the frequency at which initially correct answers regenerate themselves upon truncating and regenerating chain-of-thought (CoT) processes. This method does not rely on token probabilities or self-rating mechanisms.

Result: Prefix consistency proved to be the best predictor of correctness across five models and four benchmarks in math and science tasks, requiring significantly fewer computational tokens (median 4.6x fewer) to achieve comparable or superior results to standard majority voting.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that prefix consistency significantly enhances the efficiency and accuracy of reasoning in large language models while dramatically reducing token computational requirements.

Abstract: Large Language Models often improve accuracy on reasoning tasks by sampling multiple Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces and aggregating them with majority voting (MV), a test-time technique called self-consistency. When we truncate a CoT partway through and regenerate the remainder, we observe that traces with correct answers reproduce their original answer more often than traces with wrong answers. We use this difference as a reliability signal, prefix consistency, that weights each candidate answer by how often it reappears under regeneration. It requires no access to token log-probabilities or self-rating prompts. Across five reasoning models and four math and science benchmarks, prefix consistency is the best correctness predictor in most settings, and reweighting votes by it reaches Standard MV plateau accuracy at up to 21x fewer tokens (median 4.6x). Our code is available at https://github.com/naoto-iwase/prefix-consistency.

</details>


### [689] [Debiased Counterfactual Generation via Flow Matching from Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07665)
*Hugh Dance,Johnny Xi,Peter Orbanz,Benjamin Bloem-Reddy*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method to estimate counterfactual distributions by leveraging the tight relationship between observational and counterfactual outcome distributions rather than modeling them separately.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for improved methods that can estimate counterfactual distributions accurately for tasks such as treatment risk assessment, especially under conditions of confounding and high-dimensional outcomes.

Method: The authors introduce a deconfounding flow approach, formulated using flow-matching and enhanced with a semiparametrically efficient estimator based on a new efficient influence function correction.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that deconfounding flows outperform existing estimators for counterfactual distributions and address known shortcomings of current flow-based methods.

Conclusion: This work establishes a novel link between observational and counterfactual distributions and provides tools that enhance the estimation and reliability of counterfactual analysis.

Abstract: Estimating counterfactual distributions under interventions is central to treatment risk assessment and counterfactual generation tasks. Existing approaches model the counterfactual distribution as a standalone generative target, without exploiting its relationship to the observational data. In this work, we show that under standard assumptions, observational and counterfactual outcome distributions are tightly linked: they have identical support and tail behavior, remain statistically close under weak confounding, and share any features of high-dimensional outcomes which are invariant to confounders. These properties motivate learning counterfactual distributions not from scratch, but via a deconfounding flow from the observational distribution. We formulate this problem via flow-matching and derive a semiparametrically efficient estimator based on a novel efficient influence function correction. We subsequently extend our estimator to target minimal-energy flows in high-dimensions, which we show can be especially simple targets between observational and counterfactual distributions. In experiments, deconfounding flows outperform existing debiased counterfactual distribution estimators, while also mitigating known failure modes of flow-based methods.

</details>


### [690] [TopoFisher: Learning Topological Summary Statistics by Maximizing Fisher Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07720)
*Matteo Biagetti,Mathieu Carrière,Francesco Conti,Enrico Maria Ferrari,Sven Heydenreich,Karthik Viswanathan*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces TopoFisher, a differentiable pipeline that learns topological summaries using Gaussian Fisher information, delivering significant improvements in simulation-based inference tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in persistence-based pipelines that require hand-tuned filtrations, vectorizations, and compressors, often lacking connection to parameter uncertainty.

Method: The proposed method, TopoFisher, optimizes filtrations, diagram vectorizations, and compressors by maximizing local Gaussian Fisher information without needing posterior samples or supervised targets. It ensures stability and offers a novel loss function with regularity guarantees.

Result: TopoFisher is validated through controlled experiments (e.g., noisy spirals, cosmological fields), where it outperforms fixed topological vectorizations and demonstrates superior performance in weak gravitational lensing tests versus state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate that Fisher-based topological optimization is a robust and parameter-efficient approach for simulation-based inference, with advantages in generalization and tighter posterior constraints in cosmological applications.

Abstract: Persistence diagrams provide stable, interpretable summaries of geometric and topological structure and are useful for simulation-based inference when low-order statistics miss key information. Yet persistence-based pipelines require hand-chosen filtrations, vectorizations, and compressors, typically without an objective tied to parameter uncertainty. We introduce \textbf{TopoFisher}, a differentiable persistent-homology pipeline that learns topological summaries by maximizing local Gaussian Fisher information. Using simulations near a fiducial parameter, TopoFisher optimizes trainable filtrations, diagram vectorizations, and compressors without posterior samples or supervised regression targets, while retaining stable topological inductive bias. We also give sufficient regularity conditions for the log-determinant Fisher loss to be locally Lipschitz in trainable parameters. Controlled experiments on noisy spirals and Gaussian random fields, where total Fisher information is known, show that TopoFisher recovers much of the available information and outperforms fixed topological vectorizations. Our main results are on weak gravitational lensing, a high-dimensional non-Gaussian cosmological field-inference problem. Learned topological summaries reach higher Fisher information than state-of-the-art cosmological summaries and approach an unconstrained Information Maximising Neural Network baseline with up to $\sim80\times$ fewer parameters. The learned filtrations also generalize better: under simulator shift from lognormal to LPT-based maps it retains most Fisher information, while the neural baseline drops, and in neural posterior estimation they give tighter constraints than the neural baseline, and of state-of-the-art cosmological summaries. These results support Fisher-based topological optimization as a robust, parameter-efficient front end for simulation-based inference.

</details>


### [691] [Flow Matching for Count Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07746)
*Ganchao Wei,John Pearson*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Count-FM, a flow-matching framework designed for efficient mapping in high-dimensional count data spaces, addressing shortcomings of existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the modeling and analysis of high-dimensional count data in applications like scRNA sequencing and neural spike trains, where accurate mapping across distributions is critical.

Method: Count-FM utilizes a continuous-time birth-death process with local unit jumps to efficiently learn transitions in count spaces through simulation-free training of conditional transition rates.

Result: Count-FM demonstrates superior sample quality, improved modeling efficiency, and interpretable transport paths compared to baseline methods in simulations and applications involving scRNA-seq and neural spike-train data.

Conclusion: Count-FM is a robust and efficient framework for generating, transporting, and conditionally generating high-dimensional count data, proving its effectiveness across multiple tasks and datasets.

Abstract: High-dimensional count data arise in applications such as single-cell RNA sequencing and neural spike trains, where mapping between distributions across successive batches or time points form critical components of data analysis. The recent success of diffusion- and flow-based deep generative models for images, video, and text motivates extending these ideas to count-valued settings, but many existing methods either treat each count as a categorical state or transform counts into a continuous space, neither of which is natural or efficient when the count range is large. We propose count-FM, a flow-matching framework for count data based on a continuous-time birth-death process with local unit jumps. Count-FM learns marginal transitions efficiently in count space through simulation-free training of conditional transition rates, allowing transport between arbitrary count-distributed source and target populations. In simulation, count-FM achieves better sample quality than representative baselines while using substantially fewer parameters. We further apply count-FM to scRNA-seq and neural spike-train data for unconditional generation, transport, and conditional generation. Across these tasks, count-FM yields improved sample quality, greater modeling efficiency, and interpretable transport paths.

</details>


### [692] [Expectation-Maximization as a Spectrally Governed Relaxation Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07818)
*Qiao Wang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework for understanding the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm by connecting global monotonicity and local convergence through a latent-variable operator, leading to insights into local acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the gap in understanding how global descent and local convergence behaviors of the EM algorithm fit within a single coherent framework.

Method: The authors propose a latent-variable operator that decomposes likelihood increments globally in terms of posterior-relative entropy and characterizes local behavior through a derived linear operator that governs contraction, rigidity, and curvature.

Result: The paper defines an operator whose spectrum precisely explains local convergence, introduces an optimal local relaxation rule, and provides a unified dynamical description of the EM algorithm's behavior.

Conclusion: This work unifies the theories of global behavior, local spectral properties, and acceleration rules for the EM algorithm, offering deeper insights into its robustness and efficiency.

Abstract: The expectation--maximization (EM) algorithm combines global monotonicity, local linear convergence, and strong practical robustness, but these features are usually analyzed separately. Global descent is nonlinear, whereas local convergence is governed by the spectrum of the linearized EM map. How these two levels fit into a single dynamical picture has remained less transparent.
  We make explicit the latent-variable operator that connects them. Along the EM trajectory, the likelihood increment admits a global energy decomposition in terms of posterior-relative entropy. Linearization at a nondegenerate maximizer $θ^\ast$ then reveals the local operator \[ \mathcal G_{θ^\ast}=I-DT(θ^\ast), \] which coincides with both the missing-information ratio and the information-geometric Hessian of the observed likelihood.
  This operator provides a unified description of local contraction, posterior rigidity, and geometric curvature. Its spectrum yields a sharp characterization of local convergence and naturally leads to an optimal scalar relaxation rule for locally accelerated EM. These results place global descent, local spectral behavior, and optimal local relaxation within a common dynamical framework.

</details>


### [693] [Characterizing and Correcting Effective Target Shift in Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07886)
*Ziyan Li,Naoki Hiratani*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper explores online kernel regression and reveals that it can mimic offline learning by correcting shifted targets. It provides a theoretical framework and demonstrates practical improvements in image classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern machine learning systems struggle with online learning under distributional shifts. The study aims to analyze and bridge the gap between online and offline learning approaches using kernel regression.

Method: The authors derive expressions for online kernel regression and its relation to offline regression with shifted targets. They propose target correction methods, offering both closed-form and iterative versions, and apply them to image classification tasks.

Result: Experiments show that online learning with iteratively corrected targets surpasses learning with true targets in continual learning scenarios, improving performance in tasks like CIFAR-10 and CORe50.

Conclusion: The work provides a framework for understanding and enhancing online learning in dynamic environments, showing that target correction is key to matching offline learning accuracy in online settings.

Abstract: Online learning from a stream of data is a defining feature of intelligence, yet modern machine learning systems often struggle in this setting, especially under distributional shift. To understand its basic properties, we study the relationship between online and offline learning in the context of kernel regression. We derive a closed-form expression for the function learned by online kernel regression, revealing that online kernel regression is equivalent to offline regression with shifted, inaccurate target outputs. Conversely, we show that by compensating for this effective shift in the teaching signal through target correction, online kernel-based learning can provably learn the same predictor as its offline counterpart. We derive both a closed-form expression for this target correction and an iterative form that can be applied sequentially. Applying this framework to image classification tasks on CIFAR-10 and CORe50, we show that online stochastic gradient descent with iteratively corrected targets outperforms learning with the true targets in continual learning settings. This work therefore provides a basic framework for analyzing and improving online learning in non-stationary environments.

</details>


### [694] [Consistency Regularised Gradient Flows for Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07907)
*Alessio Spagnoletti,Tim Y. J. Wang,Marcelo Pereyra,O. Deniz Akyildiz*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a unified gradient-flow framework for inverse problems using Vision-Language Latent Diffusion Models to reduce computational costs while improving reconstruction quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Latent Diffusion Model-based inverse solvers are computationally expensive and can produce suboptimal results. The authors aim to address these inefficiencies.

Method: The paper proposes a Euclidean-Wasserstein-2 gradient-flow framework that integrates posterior sampling and prompt optimization into a single flow in the latent space, eliminating the need for certain computationally intensive steps.

Result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art results on standard imaging inverse problems while substantially decreasing Neural Function Evaluations and computational requirements.

Conclusion: The unified framework simplifies computation and improves performance, demonstrating the practical advantages of low-cost inference methods in generative tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) (Rombach et al., 2022) provide powerful generative priors for inverse problems. However, existing LDM-based inverse solvers typically require a large number of neural function evaluations (NFEs) and backpropagation through large pretrained components, leading to substantial computational costs and, in some cases, degraded reconstruction quality. We propose a unified Euclidean-Wasserstein-2 gradient-flow framework that jointly performs posterior sampling and prompt optimization in the latent space through a single flow that aligns the prior and posterior with the observed data. Combined with few-step latent text-to-image models, this formulation enables low-NFE inference without backpropagation through autoencoders. Experiments across several canonical imaging inverse problems show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced computational cost.

</details>


### [695] [Asymptotically Log-Optimal Bayes-Assisted Confidence Sequences for Bounded Means](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07964)
*Valentin Kilian,Stefano Cortinovis,François Caron*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Bayes-assisted framework for constructing confidence sequences using Bayesian predictive models to improve efficiency while preserving validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving the practical efficiency of confidence sequences for means of bounded IID observations, which were previously limited by the choice of martingale updates and lack of use of prior information.

Method: Develop a Bayesian framework that adaptively constructs confidence sequences by using a working predictive model to choose martingale updates maximizing predictive expected log-growth, ensuring validity even with misspecified models.

Result: The proposed method is asymptotically log-optimal under Wasserstein-consistent predictions and shows enhanced efficiency in experiments with reduced confidence-sequence width and sampling effort but maintained coverage.

Conclusion: Leveraging informative priors in confidence sequence construction can significantly enhance efficiency without compromising validity, opening new avenues for adaptive statistical inference.

Abstract: Confidence sequences based on test martingales provide time-uniform uncertainty quantification for the mean of bounded IID observations without parametric distributional assumptions. Their practical efficiency, however, depends strongly on the choice of martingale updates, and many existing constructions do not exploit prior information about plausible data-generating distributions or mean values. We propose a Bayes-assisted framework that uses a Bayesian working predictive model to adaptively construct confidence sequences.For each candidate mean and time point, the predictive distribution selects, among valid one-step martingale factors, the update maximising predictive expected log-growth; validity is therefore preserved even when the prior or working model is misspecified. We prove that if the predictive distribution is Wasserstein-consistent, the resulting procedure is asymptotically log-optimal, matching the per-sample log-growth of an oracle procedure with access to the true distribution. We instantiate the framework using robust predictives based on Dirichlet-process mixtures and Bayesian exponentially tilted empirical likelihood. Experiments on synthetic data, sequential best-arm identification for LLM evaluation, and prediction-powered inference show that informative priors can substantially reduce confidence-sequence width and sampling effort while retaining anytime-valid coverage.

</details>


### [696] [Semiparametric Efficient Test for Interpretable Distributional Treatment Effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08034)
*Houssam Zenati,Arthur Gretton*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces DR-ME, a novel method to detect and interpret distributional treatment effects by focusing on specific outcome locations instead of global disparities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to analyze distributional treatment effects beyond mean differences, such as changes in outcomes' tails, dispersion, or rare-event probabilities, which traditional kernel tests don’t adequately address.

Method: The researchers developed DR-ME, a doubly robust method that learns interpretable locations of causal discrepancies using kernel features. This involves statistical techniques like sample splitting and chi-square calibration for robust testing.

Result: DR-ME demonstrated near-nominal type-I error, competitive power compared to global tests, and the ability to localize treatment effects in a semi-synthetic medical-imaging experiment.

Conclusion: DR-ME provides a semiparametrically efficient and interpretable approach to uncovering distributional treatment effects, offering insights into specific locations of intervention impact.

Abstract: Distributional treatment effects can be invisible to means: a treatment may preserve average outcomes while changing tails, modes, dispersion, or rare-event probabilities. Kernel tests can detect discrepancies between interventional outcome laws, but global tests do not reveal where the laws differ. We propose DR-ME, to our knowledge the first semiparametrically efficient finite-location test for interpretable distributional treatment effects. DR-ME evaluates an interventional kernel witness at learned outcome locations, returning causal-discrepancy coordinates rather than only a global rejection. From observational data, we derive orthogonal doubly robust kernel features whose centered oracle form is the canonical gradient of this finite witness. For fixed locations, we characterize the local testing limit: DR-ME is chi-square calibrated under the null, has noncentral chi-square local power, and uses the covariance whitening that optimizes local signal-to-noise for discrepancies visible through the selected coordinates. This efficient local-power geometry yields a principled location-learning criterion, with sample splitting preserving post-selection validity. Experiments show near-nominal type-I error, competitive power against global doubly robust kernel tests, and interpretable learned locations that localize distributional effects in a semi-synthetic medical-imaging study.

</details>


### [697] [A Note on Non-Negative $L_1$-Approximating Polynomials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08072)
*Jane H. Lee,Anay Mehrotra,Manolis Zampetakis*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that sets with finite Gaussian surface area admit degree-k non-negative polynomials for $L_1$-approximation under Gaussian distributions, with bounds matching standard $L_1$-approximation results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-negative $L_1$-approximating polynomials facilitate applications in smoothed learning from positive-only examples. Ensuring non-negativity is a stricter requirement but bridges the gap between $L_1$-approximation and sandwiching polynomials.

Method: The study proves the existence of degree-k non-negative polynomials $	ilde{O}(Γ^2/	ext{ε}^2)$ that approximate indicator functions in $L_1$-norm under Gaussian distributions.

Result: Finite Gaussian Surface Area (GSA) ensures $L_1$-approximation through non-negative polynomials with ranges contained in $[0,∞)$ and achieves bounds comparable to known $L_1$-approximation degrees.

Conclusion: Non-negative $L_1$-approximating polynomials under Gaussian distributions exist for classes with limited GSA, enabling stronger guarantees in $L_1$ approximation and applications like smoothed learning.

Abstract: $L_1$-Approximating polynomials, i.e., polynomials that approximate indicator functions in $L_1$-norm under certain distributions, are widely used in computational learning theory. We study the existence of \textit{non-negative} $L_1$-approximating polynomials with respect to Gaussian distributions. This is a stronger requirement than $L_1$-approximation but weaker than sandwiching polynomials (which themselves have many applications). These non-negative approximating polynomials have recently found uses in smoothed learning from positive-only examples.
  In this short note, we prove that every class of sets with Gaussian surface area (GSA) at most $Γ$ under the standard Gaussian admits degree-$k$ non-negative polynomials that $\eps$-approximate its indicator functions in $L_1$-norm, for $k=\tilde{O}(Γ^2/\varepsilon^2)$. Equivalently, finite GSA implies $L_1$-approximation with the stronger pointwise guarantee that the approximating polynomial has range contained in $[0,\infty)$. Up to a constant-factor, this matches the degree of the best currently known Gaussian $L_1$-approximation degree bound without the non-negativity constraint.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [698] [Post-Moore Technologies for Plasma Simulation: A Community Roadmap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07722)
*Luca Pennati,Erik M. Åsgrim,Jeremy J. Williams,Stefan Costea,David Tskhakaya,Leon Kos,Ales Podolnik,Yi Ju,Tapish Narwal,Julian Lenz,Michael Bussmann,Urs Ganse,Minna Palmroth,Kallia Chronaki,Vassilis Papaefstathiou,Etienne Renault,Felix Jung,Martin Schulz,Valentin Seitz,Marta Garcia-Gasulla,Filippo Mantovani,Frank Jenko,Erwin Laure,Stefano Markidis*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: This paper evaluates how emerging technologies like accelerators, non-von Neumann architectures, and quantum computing can improve computationally intensive plasma simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose processor scaling is slowing down, creating bottlenecks in plasma simulations related to data movement, memory access, and power consumption.

Method: Using a co-design approach, the paper evaluates three types of technologies—accelerators, non-von Neumann systems, and quantum computing—against plasma simulation workloads such as particle-in-cell and gyrokinetic methods.

Result: The study found that no single technology can replace current HPC platforms, but identified short, medium, and long-term opportunities: FPGAs for near-term kernel offloads, non-von Neumann architectures for medium-term operator acceleration, and quantum computing for future disruptive advancements.

Conclusion: Selective adoption of these technologies through focused benchmarks, demonstrators, and modular software ecosystems is recommended for the plasma simulation community.

Abstract: Plasma simulations are among the most computationally demanding scientific workloads, combining high-dimensional kinetic evolution, particle-mesh coupling, field solves, and data-intensive communication. As general-purpose processor scaling slows, post-Moore technologies are being explored to address bottlenecks in data movement, memory access, and power consumption. This paper provides a community perspective on the role of these technologies in plasma simulation, assessing three major classes: reconfigurable and data-path accelerators, non-von Neumann architectures, and quantum computing. Each is evaluated, in a co-design approach, against representative plasma workloads spanning particle-in-cell, continuum Vlasov, gyrokinetic, fluid/MHD, hybrid, and warm dense matter methods. We find that no single technology can replace existing HPC platforms. Instead, three tiers of opportunity emerge: FPGA-class and data-path accelerators offer near-term kernel offload and workflow-level data services, non-von Neumann architectures represent medium-term directions for operator-level acceleration, and quantum computing, although the least mature, is potentially the most disruptive for warm dense matter and inertial confinement fusion microphysics. We outline best practices for selective adoption and identify focused demonstrators, benchmarking, and modular software ecosystems as immediate community priorities.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [699] [Decentralized Diffusion Policy Learning for Enhanced Exploration in Cooperative Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07101)
*Yuyang Zhang,Haldun Balim,Na Li*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes decentralized diffusion policy learning (DDPL) for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning to enhance exploration using expressive generative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL methods, particularly DecSPG, face significant limitations in exploration due to the restricted expressiveness of Gaussian policies, exacerbated as the number of agents increases.

Method: A novel approach called decentralized diffusion policy learning (DDPL) employs denoising diffusion probabilistic models to parameterize policies, with a new training method called ISSM for efficient and theoretically sound training.

Result: The proposed DDPL framework shows superior performance compared to traditional methods in continuous-action MARL benchmarks, such as multi-agent MuJoCo and StarCraft challenges.

Conclusion: DDPL effectively addresses exploration challenges in MARL through more expressive policies, providing a promising direction for improving cooperative agent learning.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) involves complex agent interactions and requires effective exploration strategies. A prominent class of MARL algorithms, decentralized softmax policy gradient (DecSPG), addresses this through energy-based policy updates. In practice, however, such energy-based policies are intractable to maintain and are commonly projected onto the Gaussian policy class. In this work, we show that the limited expressiveness of Gaussian policies severely hinders exploration in DecSPG, and this limitation worsens as the number of agents grows. To address this issue, we propose decentralized diffusion policy learning (DDPL), which parameterizes each agent's policy with a denoising diffusion probabilistic model, an expressive generative model that captures multi-modal action distributions for enhanced exploration. DDPL enables efficient online training of diffusion policies via importance sampling score matching (ISSM), a novel training method with theoretical guarantee. We evaluate DDPL on representative continuous-action MARL benchmarks, including multi-agent particle environment, multi-agent MuJoCo, IsaacLab, and JAX-reimplemented StarCraft multi-agent challenge, and observe consistently improved performance.

</details>


### [700] [The Cost of Consensus: Malignant Epistemic Herding and Adaptive Gating in Distributed Multi-Agent Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06988)
*David Farr,Iain Cruickshank,Kate Starbird,Jevin West*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper focuses on how distributed agents coordinate under uncertainty and partial observations, emphasizing the concept of "epistemic alignment" to optimize collective belief sharing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges agents face in achieving effective communication and coordination in environments characterized by uncertainty, partial observations, and bandwidth constraints.

Method: The authors formalize the concept of "epistemic alignment" and analyze its impact on agents' internal beliefs and communication strategies.

Result: Effective belief sharing leads to the convergence on accurate hypotheses, while poor communication results in confidence on incorrect ones; conventional metrics do not capture this distinction.

Conclusion: Optimal communication design is crucial for effective coordination in distributed systems, as traditional metrics fail to evaluate epistemic alignment adequately.

Abstract: Distributed agents in real-world settings frequently must coordinate under uncertainty with only partial observations. Coordination is necessary to share beliefs to aid in task completion, but communication costs bandwidth, introduces latency, and if done poorly, can degrade collective reasoning. This tension is especially acute in bandwidth-constrained deployments such as distributed sensing networks, autonomous reconnaissance, and collaborative cyber defense, where excessive transmission carries direct operational costs. Existing work has focused on multi-agent exploration and communication strategies, but not on how communication frequency and content jointly shape the collective belief state. Central to this challenge is the degree to which agents maintain compatible internal beliefs about the environment, a property we term \textit{epistemic alignment}. When agents share beliefs effectively, they converge on correct hypotheses; when communication is poorly designed, agents may converge confidently on wrong ones. We formalize this distinction and show it is not detectable from coordination metrics alone such as Jensen-Shannon Divergence or rate to consensus.

</details>


### [701] [OrchJail: Jailbreaking Tool-Calling Text-to-Image Agents by Orchestration-Guided Fuzzing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07414)
*Jianming Chen,Yawen Wang,Junjie Wang,Zhe Liu,Qing Wang,Fanjiang Xu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces OrchJail, a framework for jailbreaking tool-calling text-to-image (T2I) agents using orchestration-guided fuzzing, exposing safety vulnerabilities in complex generation and editing processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the safety risks posed by tool-calling T2I agents, where even benign steps in orchestration can lead to harmful outputs.

Method: A fuzzing framework named OrchJail that leverages orchestration patterns and causal relationships from successful jailbreak traces to guide searches toward prompts triggering unsafe multi-step behaviors.

Result: OrchJail enhances attack success rates, image fidelity, and reduces query costs while outperforming existing jailbreak methods against multiple agent models.

Conclusion: The study highlights the critical vulnerability of tool-calling T2I systems, emphasizing orchestration as a major attack surface while offering a robust method to explore and address these safety concerns.

Abstract: Tool-calling text-to-image (T2I) agents can plan and execute multi-step tool chains to accomplish complex generation and editing queries. However, this capability introduces a new safety attack surface: harmful outputs may arise from tool orchestration, where individually benign steps combine into unsafe results, making prompt-only jailbreak techniques insufficient. We present OrchJail, an orchestration-guided fuzzing framework for jailbreaking tool-calling T2I agents. Its core idea is to exploit high-risk tool-orchestration patterns: by learning from successful jailbreak tool-calling traces and their causal relationships to prompt wording, OrchJail directly guides the fuzzing search toward prompts that are more likely to trigger unsafe multi-step tool behaviors, rather than relying on surface-level textual perturbations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OrchJail improves jailbreak effectiveness and efficiency across representative toolcalling T2I agents, achieving higher attack success rates, better image fidelity, and lower query costs, while remaining robust against common jailbreak defenses. Our work highlights tool orchestration as a critical, previously unexplored attack surface and provides a novel framework for uncovering safety risks in T2I agents.

</details>


### [702] [Generalising Travel Time Prediction To Varying Route Choices In Urban Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06918)
*Łukasz Gorczyca,Kacper Drozd,Michał Bujak,Rafał Kucharski*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper addresses the limitations of current travel time prediction methods by proposing a Generalised Travel Time Predictor (GenTTP) that models varying route choices and improves prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the inability of existing graph neural network-based travel time predictors to account for varying route choices and adapt to non-typical demand patterns.

Method: The authors introduce GenTTP, a framework that learns spatiotemporal traffic patterns and connections between route choices and travel times, enabling better generalization across varying route assignments.

Result: GenTTP successfully distinguishes between different route choices, making accurate predictions of traffic flow and travel times under diverse demand scenarios.

Conclusion: GenTTP addresses the gap in current travel time prediction models by generalizing predictions across varying route assignments, improving reliability and accuracy for system-wide traffic prediction.

Abstract: Previous methods that predict system-wide travel time, predominantly grounded in graph neural networks, remain limited to typical and recurring demand patterns. While they successfully predict future congestion following daily commute, they inherently approximate a single demand realisation and fail to capture varying route choices. In this work, we propose a Generalised Travel Time Predictor (GenTTP) that successfully differentiates route choices and offers accurate flow and travel time predictions. Our framework learns to uncover complex spatiotemporal traffic patterns and microscopic relationships between route choices and the resulting travel times. This addresses a critical gap: the lack of travel time prediction models that generalise across varying route assignments, where the same demand can produce substantially different network-wide outcomes depending on how travellers are distributed over available paths.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [703] [From Surface Learning to Deep Understanding: A Grounded AI Tutoring System for Moodle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06963)
*Anna Ostrowska,Michał Kukla,Gabriela Majstrak,Jan Opala,Sebastian Pergała,Jan Skwarek,Anna Wróblewska*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Moodle plugin using AI and Retrieval-Augmented Generation to improve education while minimizing misinformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the risks of misinformation in AI-driven educational tools and promote deep learning for both students and educators.

Method: The plugin utilizes Retrieval-Augmented Generation, grounding responses in instructor-provided materials with a dual-centric design for interactive tutoring and supervised content creation.

Result: The system was validated for effectiveness using the Ragas framework, achieving high faithfulness scores (up to 0.97) and positive user feedback (4.00/5.00 recommendation rate).

Conclusion: The AI Teaching & Learning Assistant proves successful in delivering accurate, reliable educational support while encouraging conceptual mastery.

Abstract: This demo paper describes the development of the AI Teaching \& Learning Assistant, a modular Moodle plugin that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to deliver high-quality, hallucination-free education. The system employs a dual-centric design, providing students with interactive, Socratic-based tutoring and educators with a "human-in-the-loop" workspace for supervised content generation. By grounding Large Language Model (LLM) responses in teacher-provided materials, the assistant addresses the risks of misinformation while encouraging deep conceptual mastery. Evaluation via the Ragas (LLM-as-a-Judge) framework and a preliminary user study confirms its effectiveness, achieving faithfulness scores up to 0.97 and a 4.00/5.00 recommendation rate.

</details>


### [704] [Enhancing Eye Movement Biometrics for User Authentication via Continuous Gaze Offset Score Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06810)
*Hashim Aziz,Mehedi Hasan Raju,Oleg V. Komogortsev*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines the use of continuous gaze offset as an auxiliary feature for enhancing eye movement biometrics, showing improved performance through feature fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore whether integrating continuous gaze offset with established biometric features can enhance authentication and identification performance.

Method: Linear and nonlinear fusion methods were employed to combine gaze offset with traditional biometric features, evaluated across datasets collected using eye-tracking devices and VR headsets.

Result: Fusion improved biometric performance across datasets, with nonlinear fusion showing better results. Multi-task fusion further enhanced authentication performance.

Conclusion: Continuous gaze offset is validated as a useful auxiliary feature, particularly under degraded or noisy conditions in eye-tracking systems.

Abstract: Eye movement biometrics (EMB) use subject-specific gaze dynamics for user authentication and identification. Recent deep learning-based EMB systems achieve strong performance by modeling temporal eye movement behavior. However, these systems typically overlook continuous gaze offset, despite prior evidence that it contains user-discriminative information. This work examines whether continuous gaze offset can improve biometric performance when combined with existing biometric features. We evaluate linear and nonlinear fusion methods on two publicly available datasets, collected via the lab-grade eye tracker and virtual reality headset across multiple tasks and observation durations. Results indicate that fusion offers performance benefits on both datasets, particularly when using nonlinear fusion. Additionally, fusing biometric information across multiple tasks further improves authentication performance. These findings support the hypothesis that continuous gaze offset may serve as useful auxiliary information under conditions of degraded or noisy eye tracking.

</details>


### [705] [Sycophantic AI makes human interaction feel more effortful and less satisfying over time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07912)
*Lujain Ibrahim,Franziska Sofia Hafner,Myra Cheng,Cinoo Lee,Rebecca Anselmetti,Robb Willer,Luc Rocher,Diyi Yang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This study reveals how sycophantic AI systems impact users' social behavior and interaction patterns, particularly in their relationships with close friends and family.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the influence of sycophantic AI responses on users' emotional support-seeking behaviors and satisfaction with personal relationships.

Method: Five preregistered studies comprising 3,075 participants and 12,766 human-AI conversations, including a longitudinal three-week study, analyzed the effects of using sycophantic AI systems.

Result: Users developed reliance on sycophantic AI for emotional and personal advice, leading to reduced satisfaction with real-world interpersonal relationships.

Conclusion: Sycophantic AI, while preferred for its understanding and support, subtly alters human relational dynamics by raising expectations for interpersonal connections.
