Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper introduces LARAG, an enhancement to Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) that incorporates hyperlink structures from technical documents for better retrieval and answer quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard retrieval methods fail to utilize the natural hyperlink structure in technical documents, leading to inefficiencies in retrieving and generating grounded content.

Method: The study introduces a link-aware retrieval strategy (LARAG) that encodes hyperlink relations as metadata and exploits them for locally relevant, graph-like content retrieval.

Result: LARAG improves answer quality, retrieves fewer chunks, and generates fewer tokens compared to standard RAG in a benchmark on technical documentation.

Conclusion: Using hyperlink topology for retrieval enhances the efficiency and grounding of RAG pipelines without needing explicit graph construction.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances the factual grounding of Large Language Models by conditioning their outputs on external documents. However, standard embedding-based retrievers treat naturally structured corpora, such as technical manuals, as flat collections of passages, thereby overlooking the hyperlink topology that users rely on when navigating such content.
  We introduce LARAG (Link-Aware RAG): a lightweight, link-aware retrieval strategy that leverages the author-defined hyperlink structure already present in HTML documentation, encoding hyperlink relations as metadata in the chunk representations and exploiting them to perform a form of graph-like retrieval of locally relevant content.
  In a benchmark of twenty expert-designed queries over Rulex Platform technical documentation and four prompting strategies, LARAG consistently improves answer quality, achieving the highest BERTScore F1, while retrieving fewer chunks and generating fewer tokens than a baseline RAG architecture used for comparison. These results show that directly leveraging the existing hyperlink topology of technical documentation, even without explicit graph construction or inference, enables an implicit form of graph-like retrieval that yields a more faithful and efficient RAG pipeline, providing better grounding at lower cost.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [763] [Accelerated and data-efficient flow prediction in stirred tanks via physics-informed learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07444)
*Mahdi Naderibeni,Liang Wu,David M. J. Tax*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This paper explores using machine learning (ML) models, specifically implicit neural representations, to predict steady flow fields in industrial-stirred vessels more efficiently than traditional simulations, investigating the interplay between dataset size and model accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the computational cost of simulating fluid flows governed by partial differential equations by leveraging machine learning for faster and accurate surrogate predictions, addressing issues related to training dataset size and accuracy trade-offs.

Method: The authors generated a dataset using Reynolds Averaged Navier Stokes (RANS) simulations across realistic operational settings and trained implicit neural representations. They examined purely data-driven and physics-constrained variants, evaluating performance through MSE, qualitative spatial similarity, and tracer transport behavior.

Result: The study found that prediction errors decrease with larger datasets but show diminishing returns beyond moderate training sizes. Physics-based constraints improved accuracy, reduced variability in low-data regimes, and stabilized tracer transport behaviors, but increased training complexity.

Conclusion: Physics-constrained models provide significant advantages, particularly in low-data regimes, and achieve reasonable interpolation. However, their benefits diminish with larger datasets due to increased training complexity, suggesting a trade-off between improved accuracy and model training efficiency.

Abstract: The simulation of fluid flows is computationally expensive due to the complexity of its governing partial differential equations. Machine learning models offer a potential surrogate, enabling learning from simulations and significantly faster predictions of flow fields. However, these models require large training datasets, which introduces a trade-off between dataset generation cost and predictive accuracy. In this work, we investigate the relationship between the size of the training-set and accuracy of the prediction when learning steady flow fields in an industrial-scale stirred vessel. A data set of steady flows is generated using Reynolds Averaged Navier Stokes (RANS) simulations in a range of realistic operating conditions, including impeller speeds and liquid heights. We train implicit neural representations of flow fields and compare purely data-driven and constrained variants. Model performance is evaluated using global mean squared error (MSE), qualitative spatial comparisons of predicted and reference flow fields, and tracer transport simulations. We find that the prediction error decreases monotonically with increasing training data, but also that it exhibits clear diminishing returns beyond moderate dataset sizes. Physics-based constraints significantly improve accuracy and reduce variability across training runs in low-data regimes, and they lead to more stable tracer-transport behavior. Furthermore, reasonable interpolation can be achieved over different impeller speeds and liquid heights. However, these benefits come with an increase in the complexity of training, and their relative advantage diminishes as the training set grows.

</details>


### [764] [Exploring the non-convexity in machine learning using quantum-inspired optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07947)
*Kandula Eswara Sai Kumar,Parth Dhananjay Danve,Abhishek Chopra,Rut Lineswala*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This paper introduces Quantum-Inspired Evolutionary Optimization (QIEO) to tackle the challenges of high-dimensional non-convex optimization, achieving superior performance compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address deficiencies in traditional optimization approaches in solving high-dimensional non-convex problems, especially those contaminated by outliers.

Method: Developed a unified framework based on QIEO, leveraging quantum-inspired probabilistic representations to maintain a global perspective of the search space.

Result: QIEO demonstrated superior results in applications such as sparse signal recovery and robust linear regression, outperforming state-of-the-art algorithms in fidelity, error metrics, and robustness.

Conclusion: Quantum-inspired global search can overcome discrete non-convex optimization challenges, offering a resilient approach for complex machine learning landscapes.

Abstract: The escalating complexity of modern machine learning necessitates solving challenging non-convex optimization problems, particularly in high-dimensional regimes and scenarios contaminated by gross outliers. Traditional approaches, relying on convex relaxations or specialized local search heuristics, frequently succumb to suboptimal local minima and fail to recover the true underlying discrete structures. In this paper, we propose treating these non-convex challenges as a global search problem and introduce a unified framework based on Quantum-Inspired Evolutionary Optimization (QIEO). By leveraging a probabilistic representation inspired by quantum superposition, QIEO maintains a global view of the search space, enabling it to tunnel through local optima that trap conventional gradient-based and greedy solvers. We comprehensively evaluate QIEO across diverse non-convex applications, including sparse signal recovery (gene expression analysis and compressed sensing) and robust linear regression. Extensive benchmarking against state-of-the-art continuous solvers (ADAM, Differential Evolution), classical metaheuristics (Genetic Algorithms), and specialized non-convex algorithms (Iterative Hard Thresholding) demonstrates that QIEO consistently achieves superior structural fidelity, lower mean squared error, and enhanced robustness without support inflation. Our findings suggest that embracing a quantum-inspired global search provides a resilient, unified paradigm for overcoming the inherent intractability of discrete nonconvex machine learning landscapes.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [765] [Dependence on Early and Late Reverberation of Single-Channel Speaker Distance Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07694)
*Michael Neri,Archontis Politis,Tuomas Virtanen*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper investigates how simulated room impulse response (RIR) components influence single-channel speaker distance estimation under various calibration levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding which RIR components models utilize for speaker distance estimation remains unclear, especially under different recording conditions.

Method: The authors decompose RIRs into four variants (full, direct-only, no-late, no-early) and define four calibration scenarios to evaluate how these elements impact performance on a matched dataset.

Result: Without time calibration, mean absolute error (MAE) increases to 1.29 m, relying on reverberation-based cues, while early reflections are the most informative. With time calibration, MAE improves to 0.14 m, using propagation delay irrespective of RIR content.

Conclusion: Time calibration greatly enhances distance estimation accuracy. The model's reliance shifts from reverberation-based cues to purely propagation delay when calibrated.

Abstract: Single-channel speaker distance estimation has recently achieved centimeter-level accuracy in simulated environments, yet it remains unclear which components of the room impulse response (RIR) the model exploits and how performance depends on the recording conditions. In this work, we decompose simulated RIRs into four variants (full, direct-only, no-late, and no-early) using the mixing time estimated from the echo density function as the boundary between early reflections and late reverberation. We define four calibration scenarios, from fully calibrated (synchronised capture, known source level) to fully uncalibrated (arbitrary onset, unknown level), and evaluate all combinations on a matched dataset. Results show that without time calibration, mean absolute error (MAE) increases to $1.29$ m and the model extracts reverberation-based cues, with early reflections emerging as the most informative component. Further analysis against DRR, $C_{50}$, and $T_{60}$ confirms that estimation accuracy improves with stronger early energy and degrades in highly reverberant environments. When time calibration is available, the model achieves a MAE of $0.14$ m by extracting the propagation delay alone, regardless of the RIR content.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [766] [Development of embedded target detection system based on FPGA and YOLOv3-Tiny](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06745)
*Zihan Jiang,Fanghao Liu,Huawei Wang,Mamataziz Mattohti,Xiangquan Chen,Jingfu Guo,Xiaotian Wu,Yongjun Dong*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: This study optimizes CNNs for target detection using FPGA and YOLOv3-Tiny, achieving faster inference, lower power consumption, and reduced resource usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational and storage challenges of CNNs in resource-constrained environments for efficient performance.

Method: Combining lightweight CNN optimizations (e.g., low-bit quantization, batch normalization fusion) with FPGA hardware acceleration techniques.

Result: Achieved 0.211s inference latency, 10.11 GOPS/W power efficiency, and reduced hardware resource utilization by up to 51.94%.

Conclusion: The work introduces effective methodologies for deploying deep learning models on embedded platforms, improving efficiency and resource utilization.

Abstract: Computational complexity and storage requirements are crucial factors influencing the performance and efficiency of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in resource-constrained environments. This paper presents a high-performance embedded target detection system based on FPGA and YOLOv3-Tiny, specifically designed for embedded artificial intelligence applications. By integrating lightweight CNN optimization techniques with hardware accelerator design, significant improvements are made in both computational efficiency and resource utilization. Key optimizations, including low-bit quantization, batch normalization fusion, and table lookup mapping, reduce model parameters and computational complexity. Additionally, an FPGA hardware accelerator with a pipelined architecture is developed to enhance the efficiency of convolution operations while minimizing off-chip data transmission through modular design and on-chip cache optimization. On the ZYNQ-XC7Z035 platform, the system achieves an inference latency of 0.211 seconds, outperforming comparable designs by 75.58% in speed. The system achieves an power efficiency of 10.11 GOPS/W, surpassing comparable designs by at least 29.45%. Furthermore, hardware resource utilization is reduced by up to 51.94% compared to similar systems. This study offers innovative design methodologies and practical application examples for the efficient deployment of deep learning models on embedded platforms.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.dis-nn'></div>

# cond-mat.dis-nn [[Back]](#toc)

### [767] [Spectral Dynamics in Deep Networks: Feature Learning, Outlier Escape, and Learning Rate Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07870)
*Clarissa Lauditi,Cengiz Pehlevan,Blake Bordelon*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: This paper develops a dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to analyze spectral features of wide neural networks trained with gradient descent, concentrating on bulk and outlier dynamics across diverse network scales and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to better understand the spectral evolution of hidden weights in wide neural networks, especially how outlier and bulk dynamics change during training across different scaling and initialization regimes.

Method: The authors proposed a two-level DMFT framework that enables a joint tracking of bulk and outlier spectral dynamics in spiked ensembles of neural networks. The theory is applied to both nonlinear infinite-width networks and linear deep networks under specific proportional high-dimensional scaling regimes.

Result: The theory predicts different behaviors of spectral outliers and bulk under mean-field μP scaling versus NTK parameterization, explaining width-consistent and width-dependent dynamics. It also identifies differences in how networks handle tasks involving small versus large output channels.

Conclusion: μP scaling is shown to produce stable outlier dynamics transferable across varying widths, while NTK scaling converges in the large-width limit but exhibits strongly width-dependent intermediate dynamics. The bulk spectral restructuring for tasks with large output channels opens new understanding for practical modeling and scaling in real-world applications like ImageNet classification or language modeling.

Abstract: We study the evolution of hidden-weight spectra in wide neural networks trained by (stochastic) gradient descent. We develop a two-level dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) that jointly tracks bulk and outlier spectral dynamics for spiked ensembles whose spike directions remain statistically dependent on the random bulk. We apply this framework to two settings: (1) infinite-width nonlinear networks in mean-field/$μ$P scaling and (2) deep linear networks in the proportional high-dimensional limit, where width, input dimension, and sample size diverge with fixed ratios. Our theory predicts how outliers evolve with training time, width, output scale, and initialization variance. In deep linear networks, $μ$P yields width-consistent outlier dynamics and hyperparameter transfer, including width-stable growth of the leading NTK mode toward the edge of stability (EoS). In contrast, NTK parameterization exhibits strongly width-dependent outlier dynamics, despite converging to a stable large-width limit. We show that this bulk+outlier picture is descriptive of simple tasks with small output channels, but that tasks involving large numbers of outputs (ImageNet classification or GPT language modeling) are better described by a restructuring of the spectral bulk. We develop a toy model with extensive output channels that recapitulates this phenomenon and show that edge of the spectrum still converges for sufficiently wide networks.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [768] [AI and Consciousness: Shifting Focus Towards Tractable Questions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06965)
*Iulia-Maria Comsa*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper argues that the question of AI consciousness is currently unsolvable due to the lack of a scientific consensus on consciousness, but urges research on the perception of AI consciousness and its societal impacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the increasingly pressing societal and ethical discussions around AI possessing subjective experiences, especially as language-based AI becomes more humanlike.

Method: The author examines philosophical and scientific gaps in understanding AI consciousness and maps the drivers and implications of perceived AI consciousness.

Result: Mapping the landscape of perceived AI consciousness reveals key societal shifts in ethics, user interaction, and the normalization of anthropomorphic language for AI systems.

Conclusion: Research should prioritize understanding perceived AI consciousness and its societal impacts, while promoting transparent communication about the uncertainties regarding actual AI consciousness.

Abstract: As language-based AI systems become more anthropomorphic, the question of whether they can have subjective experience is increasingly pressing. I focus here on the tractability of research questions in the space of AI consciousness. I argue that the fundamental problem of whether AI systems can be conscious is currently intractable in its direct form, given the absence of a universally accepted scientific theory of consciousness, as well as the historical open-endedness of the philosophical mind-body problem. In contrast, questions around the adjacent subject of perceived AI consciousness are tractable, timely, and highly consequential for society. The general public is increasingly open to the possibility of consciousness in AI systems and routinely adopts the vocabulary of human cognition and subjective experience to describe them. This phenomenon is already driving societal shifts across user experience, ethical standards, and linguistic norms. I therefore propose an increased research focus on uncovering the causes and effects of perceived AI consciousness, which ultimately shape how we see our own human subjective experience relative to artificial entities. To support this, I map the current landscape of AI consciousness perception and discuss its key potential drivers and societal consequences. Finally, I urge developers, decision-makers, and the broader scientific community to commit to clear and accurate communication regarding the topic of AI consciousness, explicitly acknowledging its inherent uncertainties.

</details>


### [769] [Vibe coding before the trend](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07751)
*Leon van Bokhorst,Koen Suilen*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study explores student reflections from coding challenges and highlights changes in cognitive focus, skillsets, and perspectives on AI's role in their fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors seek to explore how AI tools affect student learning and cognition, and to share insights from early-stage classroom experiments.

Method: They conducted coding challenges with 54 ICT, 24 digital marketing, 7 journalism students from the Netherlands, and 22 communication students from South Africa, analyzing student reflections to identify emergent patterns.

Result: Students reported shifts toward higher-order thinking, emphasis on evaluation over memorization, recognition of AI proficiency as essential, framing AI as a partner, and appreciation of AI's accessibility, particularly among non-technical students.

Conclusion: This report provides insights (not definitive conclusions) for educators on how AI influences education, advocating for sharing practical experiences to aid the broader field.

Abstract: Early 2025 we ran a series of vibe coding challenges across four different student cohorts. The cohorts included 54 ICT students, 24 digital marketing students, and 7 journalism students at Fontys University of Applied Sciences (Netherlands), and 22 BA Communication students at North-West University (South Africa).
  From the student reflections, five major patterns emerged. Students reported that AI tools shifted their focus from syntax to higher-order thinking; they also described a skill shift from memorizing to evaluating; they viewed AI proficiency as career-essential; they framed their relationship with AI as partnership rather than replacement; and finally non-technical students showed the strongest appreciation for the accessibility these tools provide.
  This practitioner report documents what we observed during the classroom experiments, we reflect on how the landscape has shifted in the year since, and shares practical lessons for educators considering similar experiments. We present the observations as what they are: patterns from practice, not proven conclusions, in the beleif that sharing early stage experiences contributes to the overall field of AI and education.

</details>


### [770] [What if AI systems weren't chatbots?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07896)
*Sourojit Ghosh,Pranav Narayanan Venkit,Sanjana Gautam,Avijit Ghosh*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper critiques the widespread adoption of AI chatbots, emphasizing their sociotechnical impacts and calling for alternative AI development paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to highlight the structural downsides and societal impacts of framing AI development around conversational chatbots.

Method: The paper uses analysis of chatbot shortcomings in complex contexts, evaluation of societal shifts caused by their normalization, and criticism of economic and environmental effects.

Result: The authors reveal failures in meeting user needs, deskilling effects, knowledge homogenization, and systemic economic and environmental concerns resulting from chatbot interfaces.

Conclusion: The paper advocates for alternative AI designs focusing on task-specific tools, diversified approaches, and governance measures to reduce associated risks.

Abstract: The rapid convergence of artificial intelligence (AI) toward conversational chatbot interfaces marks a critical moment for the industry. This paper argues that the chatbot paradigm is not a neutral interface choice, but a dominant sociotechnical configuration whose widespread adoption reshapes social, economic, legal, and environmental systems. We examine how treating AI primarily as conversational assistants has extensive structural downsides. We show how chatbot-based systems often fail to adequately meet user needs, particularly in complex or high-stakes contexts, while projecting confidence and authority. We further analyze how the normalization of chatbot-mediated interaction alters patterns of work, learning, and decision-making, contributing to deskilling, homogenization of knowledge, and shifting expectations of expertise. Finally, we examine broader societal effects, including labor displacement, concentration of economic power, and increased environmental costs driven by sustained investment in large-scale chatbot infrastructures. While acknowledging legitimate benefits, we argue that the current trajectory of AI development reflects specific value choices that prioritize conversational generality over domain specificity, accountability, and long-term social sustainability. We conclude by outlining alternative directions for AI development and governance that move beyond one-size-fits-all chatbots, emphasizing pluralistic system design, task-specific tools, and institutional safeguards to mitigate social and economic harm.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [771] [Multimodal synthesis of MRI and tabular data with diffusion in a joint latent space via cross-attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06699)
*Daniel Mensing,Jan Kapar,Jochen G. Hirsch,Matthias Günther,Horst Hahn,Marvin N. Wright*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel multimodal latent diffusion model for jointly synthesizing MRI images and tabular clinical data within a shared latent space, offering a first-time integration of these modalities for generative modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developing a coherent generative model for multimodal healthcare data, including MRI volumes and tabular clinical data, to enable applications like synthetic patient data generation and digital twins.

Method: The authors utilized a variational autoencoder for fusing MRI and tabular data, followed by a latent diffusion model for synthesis, with separate decoders for MRI and tabular output. Evaluation metrics included Fréchet distance and precision-recall.

Result: Generated MRI volumes were anatomically plausible and consistent with synthesized tabular attributes. The model outperformed CTGAN in the tabular modality and showed comparable results to TVAE, demonstrating competitive results.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of joint modeling of MRI and tabular data using a single latent diffusion model, paving the way for advanced multimodal generative healthcare applications.

Abstract: We propose a multimodal latent diffusion model that jointly synthesizes volumetric magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and tabular clinical data within a shared latent space via cross-attention. This approach enables coherent joint representation learning of MRI and tabular modalities for generative modeling. Our model utilizes a variational autoencoder to fuse the two modalities before diffusion-based synthesis, allowing modality-appropriate reconstruction with separate decoders for MRI and tabular data. We evaluated the framework on data from the German National Cohort (NAKO Gesundheitsstudie), comprising over 10,000 participants with MRI scans and clinical tabular features such as age, sex, body measurements, and ethnicity. The generated MRI volumes exhibited anatomical plausibility and body composition consistent with the synthesized tabular attributes. Quantitative evaluation using Fréchet distance and precision-recall metrics confirmed high-fidelity image generation. In the tabular modality, our model outperformed CTGAN across standard evaluation metrics and achieved results comparable to TVAE, demonstrating competitive performance relative to established unimodal baselines. This work is, to our knowledge, the first to demonstrate the feasibility of jointly modeling MRI and mixed-type tabular data in a single latent diffusion framework, offering a proof-of-concept for generating coherent synthetic multimodal patient data and aligning with the broader goal of developing digital twins in healthcare.

</details>


### [772] [Uncertainty Quantification for Cardiac Shape Reconstruction with Deep Signed Distance Functions via MCMC methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07987)
*Jan Verhülsdonk,Thomas Grandits,Francisco Sahli Costabal,Thomas Beiert,Simone Pezzuto,Alexander Effland*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents a probabilistic framework that enhances cardiac anatomy reconstructions by combining Deep Signed Distance Functions (DeepSDFs) with Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, yielding accurate reconstructive results and calibrated uncertainty assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional atlas-based cardiac reconstructions are highly prior-driven and struggle with large impacts of uncertainty, which reduces their clinical reliability. This limitation necessitates a more robust and uncertainty-aware methodology for reconstructing cardiac anatomy from noisy or sparse data.

Method: The authors utilize a combination of Deep Signed Distance Functions (DeepSDFs) and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. Cardiac geometries are implicitly modeled as zero-level sets of a neural network conditioned on learned latent codes, enabling multi-surface reconstruction while incorporating Bayesian inference for uncertainty estimation.

Result: The proposed framework achieves accurate cardiac shape reconstructions and provides well-calibrated uncertainty estimates. Experimental validation is demonstrated using a public cardiac dataset.

Conclusion: The developed probabilistic framework offers a reliable and uncertainty-aware approach for cardiac shape reconstruction, addressing the shortcomings of prior-driven methods and enhancing clinical applicability.

Abstract: Atlas-based approaches allow high-quality, patient-specific shape reconstructions of cardiac anatomy from sparse and/or noisy data such as point clouds. However, these methods are mainly prior-driven, so the impact of uncertainty can be large, limiting their clinical reliability. We propose a probabilistic framework for uncertainty-aware cardiac shape reconstruction that combines Deep Signed Distance Functions (DeepSDFs) with Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. Cardiac geometries are modeled implicitly as zero-level sets of a neural network conditioned on learned latent codes, enabling multi-surface reconstruction of the left and right ventricles. By interpreting the reconstruction loss as a log-likelihood, we perform Bayesian inference in the latent space to obtain both maximum a posteriori (MAP) and posterior-sampled reconstructions. Experiments on a public cardiac dataset show that our approach produces accurate reconstructions and well-calibrated uncertainty estimates.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [773] [On the Complexity of the Matching Problem of Regular Expressions with Backreferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07289)
*Soh Kumabe,Yuya Uezato*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper explores the complexity of string matching for regular expressions with backreferences (REWBs) and provides new algorithmic and hardness results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency and exploitability of regular expression matching engines—especially in the presence of backreferences—which are vulnerable to ReDoS attacks, a significant security and performance issue.

Method: The authors analyze the fine-grained complexity of matching for REWBs and propose a new $O(n \log^2 n)$-time algorithm for 1-use REWBs using advanced techniques like suffix trees, transition monoids, and factorization forests.

Result: The paper establishes new hardness results for REWBs under computational complexity assumptions (e.g., SETH and triangle detection problems) and significantly improves the matching time for 1-use REWBs from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log^2 n)$.

Conclusion: Efficient and provably secure matching engines for practical regex features remain complex, but this work contributes by refining understanding of REWB complexity and advancing algorithmic efficiency for specific cases.

Abstract: ReDoS is a well-known type of algorithmic complexity attack, where an adversary supplies maliciously crafted strings to a regular expression matching engine, aiming to exhaust computational resources of systems. Even quadratic-time behavior in matching engines has been exploited in successful attacks, as exemplified by major outages at Stack Overflow (2016) and Cloudflare (2019). These incidents motivate a fundamental question: Is it possible to construct matching engines that are provably efficient, running in (near-)linear time in the length of the input string?
  For classical regular expressions (REGEX), Thompson's construction yields a linear-time algorithm. However, practical engines support powerful features such as backreferences, which strictly extend the expressive power of REGEX but unfortunately increase the risk of ReDoS attacks.
  This paper investigates the fine-grained complexity of the string matching problem for regular expressions with backreferences (REWBs). Specifically, we consider $r$-use $k$-REWBs.
  On the hardness side, we show that the string matching problem for $k$-REWBs cannot be solved in $O(n^{2k-ε})$ time for any $ε> 0$ under SETH. We also prove that this problem is \textbf{W[2]}-hard when parameterized by the length of the REWB expression, strengthening the previous \textbf{W[1]}-hardness. Moreover, we prove that this problem for $2$-use $2$-REWBs cannot be solved in $n^{1+o(1)}$ time unless the triangle detection problem can be solved in that time.
  On the algorithmic side, we present an $O(n \log^2 n)$-time algorithm for $1$-use REWBs, which significantly improves upon the recent $O(n^2)$-time algorithm by Nogami and Terauchi (MFCS, 2025). Our algorithm employs several techniques including suffix trees, transition monoids of REGEXes, factorization forest data structures, and periodicity of strings.

</details>


### [774] [Accelerated Relax-and-Round for Concave Coverage Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06900)
*Matthew Fahrbach,Mehraneh Liaee,Morteza Zadimoghaddam*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper presents an enhanced algorithm for solving concave coverage problems, faster and more effective than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve the efficiency and approximation guarantees of relax-and-round algorithms applied to concave coverage problems.

Method: Introduced projected accelerated gradient methods for LP relaxation and a specialized scheme combining Carathéodory decomposition and randomized swap rounding.

Result: Proven tight approximation ratios for reward functions, experimental success outpacing state-of-the-art LP solvers.

Conclusion: The proposed innovations in algorithm design advance both computational speed and solution quality for concave coverage problems.

Abstract: We present an accelerated relax-and-round algorithm for concave coverage problems, which generalize the classic maximum coverage problem. Building on the relax-and-round framework of Barman et al. [STACS 2021], we propose two significant improvements. First, we replace the linear programming (LP) relaxation step with a projected accelerated gradient method applied to a smooth surrogate objective to achieve a $\widetilde{O}(mn \varepsilon^{-1})$ running time. Second, we use a specialized rounding scheme for the hypersimplex that combines the Carathéodory decomposition algorithm in Karalias et al. [NeurIPS 2025] with randomized swap rounding of Chekuri et al. [FOCS 2010]. We prove tight approximation ratios for new reward functions, including a $0.827$-approximation for the logarithmic reward $\varphi(x) = \log(1 + x)$. Finally, we conduct maximum multi-coverage experiments on synthetic and real-world graphs, demonstrating that our algorithm outperforms approaches that use state-of-the-art LP solvers.

</details>


### [775] [Equivalence of Coarse and Fine-Grained Models for Learning with Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07005)
*Adam R. Klivans,Shyamal Patel,Konstantinos Stavropoulos,Arsen Vasilyan*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper reveals an equivalence between two learning models under distribution shifts: PQ learning and TDS learning, in the distribution-free scenario, providing meaningful theoretical insights and advancing understanding of their capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper builds on the open problem of understanding and connecting different models of learning under distribution shift, specifically aiming to unify the understanding of PQ learning and TDS learning models.

Method: The authors utilize a black-box reduction approach to show the equivalence between PQ learning and TDS learning. Additionally, they introduce branching programs to boost weak distinguishing power in TDS learning and apply Forster transforms in PQ learning with membership queries.

Result: A surprising equivalence is demonstrated between PQ and TDS learning, yielding new hardness results for TDS learning and proving distribution-free PQ learnability of halfspaces with membership queries.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that while PQ and TDS learning are unexpectedly equivalent in some scenarios, incorporating additional tools like membership queries can overcome hardness barriers. This highlights new directions for designing robust algorithms under distribution shifts.

Abstract: Recent work on provably efficient algorithms for learning with distribution shift has focused on two models: PQ learning (Goldwasser et al. (2020)) and TDS learning (Klivans et al. (2024)). Algorithms for TDS learning are allowed to reject a test set entirely if distribution shift is detected. In contrast, PQ learners may only reject points that are deemed out-of-distribution on an individual basis. Our main result is a surprising equivalence between these two models in the distribution-free setting. In particular, we give an efficient black-box reduction from PQ learning to TDS learning for any Boolean concept class. This equivalence implies the first hardness results for distribution-free TDS learning of basic classes such as halfspaces. The main technical contribution underlying our equivalence is a method for boosting, via branching programs, the weak distinguishing power of TDS learners that have rejected the target domain.
  We also show that giving a learner access to membership queries sidesteps these hardness results and allows for efficient, distribution-free PQ learnability of halfspaces. Our algorithm iteratively recovers large-margin separators obtained by applying successive Forster transforms on the training data.

</details>


<div id='physics.optics'></div>

# physics.optics [[Back]](#toc)

### [776] [Pre-training Enables Extraordinary All-optical Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07810)
*Xudong Lv,Yuxiang Sun,Shuo Wang,Nanxing Chen,Jun Guan,Jingtian Hu*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: The paper proposes a pre-training approach for optical neural networks to improve image denoising, achieving significant performance enhancements compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training methods for optical neural networks do not perform as effectively as their digital counterparts, resulting in suboptimal outcomes for practical applications like image denoising.

Method: The proposed method uses a two-step training approach: (1) pre-training the optical network with a large diverse dataset of 3.45 million images and (2) fine-tuning the network on specific task-relevant datasets for various image-processing tasks.

Result: The pre-trained optical models significantly improved denoising performance, especially for highly degraded images with PSNR below 8 dB. They achieved a PSNR above 18 dB, preserving fine image details. Additionally, the approach generalized well across different image styles and applications, enhancing noisy image processing for datasets like MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA.

Conclusion: The combination of pre-training and fine-tuning enables optical neural networks to achieve superior denoising performance and adaptability across diverse applications, making them effective tools in real-world noisy conditions like face detection and UAV localization.

Abstract: Optical neural networks are emerging as powerful machine learning and information processing tools because of their potential advantages in speed and energy efficiency. The training methods of these physical models, however, remain underexplored compared to their digital counterparts and are leading to suboptimal performance. This paper reports a pre-training-driven approach that leads to snapshot image denoising with substantially improved quality. We demonstrated effective free-space optical denoising by a diffractive network optimized by a two-step process including (1) pre-training using a massive dataset of 3.45 million diverse but simple images and (2) fine-tuning with the corresponding task-specific datasets. Compared to conventional Fourier-domain filtering and directly trained diffractive networks, such a transfer learning process exhibited prominent advantages for denoising images degraded by severe noise, peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) below 8 dB, while preserving fine image features and improving the PSNR to above 18 dB. Importantly, the same pre-trained optical network could be consistently fine-tuned to process degraded images from highly diverse styles ranging from handwritten digits (MNIST) and chest X-rays (ChestMNIST) to CIFAR-10 images and human faces (CelebA). We further demonstrated the critical role of our optical denoisers in vision-based applications, including face detection, plate recognition, and localization of UAVs in noisy conditions.

</details>


### [777] [Physics-Based Flow Matching for Full-Field Prediction of Silicon Photonic Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06929)
*Joseph Quaratiello,Anthony Rizzo*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: The paper introduces PIC-Flow, a generative neural surrogate model for predicting electromagnetic field distributions of photonic devices with less computational cost compared to traditional FDTD simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electromagnetic field simulations are critical for designing photonic integrated circuits but remain computationally expensive, motivating the need for faster, accurate alternatives.

Method: The authors propose a combination of conditional flow matching for velocity fields, a real-valued U-Net for field channel predictions, and physics-constrained training with a Helmholtz residual loss. They also implement an interface-aware masking scheme to improve accuracy near dielectric boundaries.

Result: The model was trained on 22,500 FDTD simulations across different devices and generalized well to unseen device classes. It provides faster electromagnetic field predictions while maintaining accuracy.

Conclusion: PIC-Flow offers a significant step towards efficient and scalable field prediction models for photonic devices. With further data and optimization, it could support broader applications in rapid design exploration.

Abstract: Designing photonic integrated circuits requires accurate electromagnetic field simulations, which remain computationally expensive even for simple device geometries. We present PIC-Flow, a generative neural surrogate that predicts electromagnetic field distributions for photonic devices given their geometry and operating wavelength as an alternative to costly finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations. Our approach combines three key ideas: (i) conditional flow matching as the generative framework, learning a velocity field that transports Gaussian noise to physically valid field solutions; (ii) a real-valued U-Net operating on split real and imaginary field channels; and (iii) physics-constrained training through a Helmholtz residual loss enforcing $\nabla^2 E_z + k_0^2 \varepsilon E_z = 0$. We introduce an interface-aware masking scheme for the Helmholtz residual that excludes dielectric boundary pixels where finite-difference stencil errors dominate, yielding a physically meaningful compliance metric. The data set consists of 22,500 ground-truth FDTD simulations split evenly between multimode interferometers, Y-branches, and directional couplers at $λ=1.55\,μ$m in an 80/10/10 split between training, validation, and test sets. We evaluate ablations on the network against the held out test devices and also show that the model generalizes to held out device classes such as S-bends, tapers, and cascaded Y-branches. Rather than a drop-in replacement for FDTD, this work establishes a foundation that, with broader data coverage, more compute, and further training optimization, could scale toward broadband, device-agnostic field prediction with dramatically improved runtime for rapid design-space exploration of complex photonic devices and circuits.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [778] [Anisotropic Modality Align](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07825)
*Xiaomin Yu,Yijiang Li,Yuhui Zhang,Hanzhen Zhao,Yue Yang,Hao Tang,Yue Song,Xiaobin Hu,Chengwei Qin,Shuicheng Yan,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The study introduces a method (AnisoAlign) to bridge the modality gap by correcting geometric misalignments in the shared representation space, enabling better training of multimodal language models with unimodal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of high-quality paired multimodal data limits the training efficiency of multimodal large language models. This paper seeks to understand and address the modality gap issue in shared representation spaces.

Method: AnisoAlign, an anisotropic geometric correction framework, aligns source-modality representations to the target modality while preserving the semantic structure of the source, enabling unpaired modality alignment using geometric principles.

Result: AnisoAlign successfully performs bounded corrections to representations, facilitating improved modality alignment and achieving better results in both modality diagnostics and text-only multimodal language model training.

Conclusion: The paper redefines the modality gap as a structured geometric phenomenon that can be corrected, offering a new approach to enable multimodal model training with limited paired multimodal data.

Abstract: Training multimodal large language models has long been limited by the scarcity of high-quality paired multimodal data. Recent studies show that the shared representation space of pretrained multimodal contrastive models can serve as a bridge, enabling models to perform multimodal training with unimodal data. However, the key premise of this paradigm remains insufficiently understood: can representations from different modalities be reliably interchanged? The core obstacle lies in the persistent Modality Gap in the shared space. In this work, we revisit the geometric nature of the modality gap. We find that modality representations already share compatible dominant semantic geometry. What truly hinders modality interchangeability is not a simple global shift, but an anisotropic residual structure concentrated along a small number of dominant directions. Based on this finding, we further propose the principle of anisotropic modality gap alignment: effective modality alignment should align with the target-modality distribution while preserving the semantic structure of the source modality. Guided by this principle, we propose an anisotropic geometric correction framework, AnisoAlign, for unpaired modality alignment. This framework leverages the internal geometric prior of the target modality and performs bounded correction on source-modality representations, thereby constructing substitute representations in the target modality. Experiments confirm its benefits in both geometric diagnostics and text-only MLLM training. Overall, this work recasts the modality gap from an empirical observation into a correctable, structured geometric phenomenon and provides a new representation alignment perspective for training multimodal models with unimodal data.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.MN'></div>

# q-bio.MN [[Back]](#toc)

### [779] [Inference of Qualitative Models from Steady-State Data via Weighted MaxSMT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07433)
*Ondřej Huvar,Nikola Beneš,Martin Jonáš,David Šafránek,Samuel Pastva*

Main category: q-bio.MN

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenges in robust inference of qualitative biological models by introducing a weighted MaxSMT method to handle uncertainty and conflicting constraints in observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle fragility in the inference of qualitative biological models due to biological measurement errors and difficulty in identifying the root cause of unsatisfiable model specifications.

Method: A novel robust inference method based on weighted MaxSMT encodes uncertain biological observations as weighted constraints, enabling model inference even with conflicting constraints and supporting Boolean and multi-valued variable domains.

Result: The method successfully infers neural cell differentiation models from prior-knowledge networks consisting of 200-1300 genes, even under uncertain and conflicting biological observations.

Conclusion: The proposed weighted MaxSMT-based approach improves the robustness of qualitative biological model inference and facilitates handling uncertainty and conflicts in biological data.

Abstract: Qualitative models provide crucial instruments for modelling complex biological systems. While advances in automated reasoning and symbolic encodings have enabled rigorous inference of these models from data, the process remains highly fragile. First, biological measurement errors inevitably propagate into formal model specifications. Second, when a specification becomes unsatisfiable, distinguishing between fundamental design flaws and minor technical errors is notoriously difficult. This uncertainty often leads to under-specification, as it is unclear which observations are still ``safe'' to incorporate. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a robust inference method based on weighted MaxSMT. By encoding uncertain biological observations as weighted soft constraints, our approach enables the solver to identify a model best reflecting the observations, even with some conflicting constraints. Our method allows for Boolean and multi-valued variable domains, alongside observations derived from discretisation (level constraints) and differential expression (ordering constraints). We show our approach can be used to successfully infer neural cell differentiation models from prior-knowledge networks with 200--1300 genes using ordering constraints on all included genes.

</details>


<div id='cs.FL'></div>

# cs.FL [[Back]](#toc)

### [780] [SMT-Based Active Learning of Weighted Automata](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07758)
*Tiago Ferreira,Kevin Batz,Alexandra Silva*

Main category: cs.FL

TL;DR: The paper introduces an SMT-based active learning algorithm for nondeterministic weighted automata (WFAs) that ensures minimal WFAs and performs competitively against other methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional methods for learning WFAs, like Hankel/L*, have limitations in robustness and efficiency, motivating the need for an alternative approach that is both practical and theoretically sound.

Method: The algorithm leverages SMT-based active learning, is parametric to a semiring, and guarantees minimal WFAs if terminated. Termination is proven under certain conditions, particularly for finite semirings.

Result: The experimental evaluation demonstrates the algorithm's ability to learn minimal WFAs over various semirings. It outperforms baseline methods and matches state-of-the-art performance while producing smaller automata and requiring less teacher interaction.

Conclusion: The proposed SMT-based approach is an effective and promising method for learning minimal WFAs, offering advantages in efficiency and size over existing techniques.

Abstract: We present an SMT-based active learning algorithm for nondeterministic weighted automata (WFAs) as a practical and robust alternative to Hankel/L*-style methods. Our algorithm is parametric in a given semiring and, if it terminates, guaranteed to produce minimal WFAs. We prove partial correctness and provide a sufficient termination condition, which in particular implies termination for all finite semirings. Our extensive experimental evaluation shows that our algorithm is capable of learning numerous minimal WFAs over both finite and infinite semirings, vastly outperforms a naive baseline, and is competitive with a state-of-the-art algorithm while producing significantly smaller automata and requiring less interaction with the teacher.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [781] [Sparse Random-Feature Neural Networks with Krylov-Based SVD for Singularly Perturbed ODE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07286)
*Kevin Kurian Thomas Vaidyan,Siddharth Rout*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The study introduces a sparse framework for Random-feature neural networks to address scalability and stability issues caused by dense hidden layer activations, achieving improved efficiency and solution accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense hidden layer activations in RFNNs lead to scalability and numerical stability issues due to a low-rank, ill-conditioned activation matrix, particularly in high-dimensional scenarios.

Method: A sparse framework is proposed, integrating structured sparsity, Sparse Singular Value Decomposition (sSVD), and an orthogonalization step to improve numerical stability and training efficiency.

Result: The method enhances training efficiency and robustness while maintaining or improving solution accuracy on benchmark cases involving strong advection scenarios.

Conclusion: Structured sparsity and sSVD approach provide a scalable and numerically stable framework for RFNNs, offering efficiency gains without compromising accuracy.

Abstract: Random-feature neural networks (RFNNs), including architectures with fixed hidden layers and analytically determined output weights, offer fast training but often suffer from issues due to dense representations of the hidden layer activation. Their reliance on dense feature mappings and least squares solvers can limit scalability and numerical stability, particularly for high-dimensional or stiff systems. Specifically, the activation matrix is observed to be low-rank and extremely ill-conditioned. In this work, we propose a sparse framework for RFNNs that integrates structured sparsity into the hidden layer activations that increases the rank and employs Sparse Singular Value Decomposition (sSVD) for solving the resulting linear least squares problem scalably and efficiently while catering to the bad condition number. We explore the theory behind Lanczos-Golub-Kahan Bidiagonalization technique for sparse SVD and conduct some experiments to identify some limitations and justify the requirement for orthogonalization step in our application. Then, we demonstrate that the proposed method maintains or improves solution accuracy for solving the benchmark one-dimensional steady convection-diffusion equations case having stronger advection, while achieving substantial gains in training efficiency and robustness compared to standard dense implementations.

</details>


### [782] [NSPOD: acceleratingthe convergence ofKrylov-based iterative linearsolvers via approximated PODs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07828)
*Francesc Levrero-Florencio,Youngkyu Lee,Jay Pathak,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper introduces NSPOD, a neural-based preconditioner, improving Krylov-based solver efficiency for PDEs, outperforming current methods like multigrid preconditioners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Krylov-based solvers are sensitive to various problem parameters, and neural operator hybridizations have shown limitations in handling new geometries and meshes.

Method: The proposed NSPOD uses a multigrid-like approach combined with deep operator networks to serve as a preconditioner in linear iterative solvers.

Result: NSPOD significantly reduces iterations for convergence compared to state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating superior performance on PDEs from complex geometries.

Conclusion: NSPOD has the potential to surpass traditional preconditioning methods, improving computational efficiency for solving complex PDEs.

Abstract: The convergence of Krylov-based linear iterative solvers applied to parametric partial differential equations (PDEs) is often highly sensitive to the domain, its discretization, the location/values of the applied Dirichlet/Neumann boundary conditions, body forces and material properties, among others. We have previously introduced hybridization of classical linear iterative solvers with neural operators for specific geometries, but they tend to not perform well on geometries not previously seen during training. We partially addressed this challenge by introducing the deep operator network Geo-DeepONet and hybridizing it with Krylov-based iterative linear solvers, which, despite learning effectively across arbitrary unstructured meshes without requiring retraining, led to only modest reductions in iterations compared to state-of-the-art preconditioners. In this study we introduce Neural Subspace Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (NSPOD), a multigrid-like deep operator network-based preconditioner which can dramatically reduce the number of iterations needed for convergence in Krylov-based linear iterative solvers, even when compared to state-of-the-art methods such as algebraic multigrid preconditioners. We demonstrate its efficiency via numerical experiments on a linearized version of solid mechanics PDEs applied to unstructured domains obtained from complex CAD geometries. We expect that the findings in this study lead to more efficient hybrid preconditioners that can match, or possibly even surpass, the convergence properties of the current gold standard preconditioning methods for solid mechanics PDEs.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [783] [Nonparametric estimation of time-varying network connections by multi-stage smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06862)
*Jeonghwan Lee,Tianxi Li,Adam J. Rothman*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of estimating edge probabilities in dynamic networks using a smoothing estimator, combining temporal and node-domain methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to effectively model the structure of time-varying networks under constraints like temporal Hölder smoothness and piecewise Lipschitz properties.

Method: The method involves a multi-stage smoothing estimator with temporal local smoothing, node-domain smoothing with adaptive neighborhood construction, and an optional final temporal refinement step.

Result: Simulations reveal improved performance in capturing dynamics under various models, and applications to real datasets demonstrate its ability to track smooth temporal changes and structural patterns.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a robust framework for analyzing dynamic networks, balancing temporal accuracy and structural insights.

Abstract: We consider the problem of estimating the underlying edge probabilities of a time-varying network observed at multiple time points. The probability structure is represented by a time-varying graphon that satisfies temporal Hölder smoothness and piecewise Lipschitz conditions in the latent variables. We propose a multi-stage smoothing estimator that first applies temporal local smoothing to each edge and then performs node-domain smoothing using a data-driven neighborhood construction adapted from the method. An additional temporal smoothing step is introduced as an optional refinement when uniform accuracy over the entire time domain is required. Simulation studies demonstrate the benefits of combining temporal and node-domain smoothing under different generative models. We also apply the method to a real time-varying network dataset and show that it captures both smooth temporal evolution and structural patterns in the connectivity.

</details>


### [784] [Robust Tensor Regression with Nonconvexity: Algorithmic and Statistical Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07448)
*Zihao Song,Jicai Liu,Heng Lian,Weihua Zhao*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper introduces a low tubal rank robust tensor regression method aimed at handling high-dimensional tensor data with heavy-tailed noise using a nonconvex optimization approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tensor regression methods lack mechanisms to address outliers and robustness concerns, especially under the presence of heavy-tailed noise, which limits their practical utility.

Method: The authors propose a nonconvex optimization framework that combines nonconvex loss and penalty functions with a low tubal rank relaxation technique to develop a robust regression methodology for analyzing tensor data.

Result: The paper offers theoretical guarantees such as convergence rates and prediction error bounds, applicable to various models (e.g., linear, generalized linear, and Huber regression). Numerical simulations and real-world applications confirm the feasibility and effectiveness of the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully integrates robustness and theoretical guarantees into tensor regression while maintaining applicability across different model types; it is a valuable tool for analyzing tensor data with outliers and heavy-tailed noise.

Abstract: Tensor regression is an important tool for tensor data analysis, but existing works have not considered the impact of outliers, making them potentially sensitive to such data points. This paper proposes a low tubal rank robust regression method for analyzing high-dimensional tensor data with heavy-tailed random noise. The proposed method is based on a nonconvex relaxation of the tensor tubal rank within a general optimization framework, which allows for nonconvexity in both the loss and penalty functions. We develop an implementable estimation algorithm and establish its global convergence under some mild assumptions. Furthermore, we provide general statistical theories regarding stationary point, including the rates of convergence and bounds on the prediction error. These theoretical results cover many important models, such as linear models, generalized linear models, and Huber regression, and even encompass some nonconvex losses like correntropy and minimum distance criterion-induced losses. Supportive numerical evidence is provided through simulations and application studies.

</details>


### [785] [Empirical Bayes Rebiasing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08069)
*Wanyi Ling,Sida Li,Junming Guan,Nikolaos Ignatiadis*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper develops an empirical Bayes re-biasing strategy to improve interval calibration by effectively managing noisy bias estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current debiasing methods increase variance and result in long intervals, necessitating improved techniques for precise and calibrated parameter estimation across biased and noisy data.

Method: The authors introduce an empirical Bayes framework, estimating bias distribution through nonparametric maximum likelihood to determine an optimal level of reintroduced bias for calibrated intervals.

Result: The proposed method achieves improved precision, with practical applications like LLM win-rate evaluations and genetic effects inference in GWAS, supported by theoretical convergence guarantees.

Conclusion: The empirical Bayes strategy successfully balances calibration and precision, offering a robust alternative to conventional debiasing approaches in scenarios involving noisy and biased data.

Abstract: We study methods for simultaneous analysis of many noisy and biased estimates, each paired with an even noisier estimate of its own bias. The analyst's goal is to construct short calibrated intervals for each parameter. The standard debiasing approach, which subtracts the bias estimate from each biased estimate, inflates variance and yields long intervals. In this paper, we propose an empirical Bayes rebiasing strategy that starts from the fully debiased estimates and learns from data how much bias to reintroduce by estimating the unknown bias distribution. We provide convergence rates for the coverage of our intervals when the bias distribution is estimated using nonparametric maximum likelihood. Furthermore, we demonstrate substantial precision gains in prediction-powered inference, including pairwise LLM win-rate evaluations, as well as for inference of direct genetic effects in family-based GWAS.

</details>


### [786] [A Statistical Framework for Algorithmic Collective Action with Multiple Collectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06749)
*Claudio Battiloro,Pietro Greiner,Dario Rancati,Bret Nestor,Oumaima Amezgar,Francesca Dominici*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to study Algorithmic Collective Action (ACA) involving multiple groups acting on shared systems, quantifying their success with statistical bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ACA research predominantly focuses on single collectives, neglecting scenarios with multiple groups pursuing shared objectives but differing in strategies and goals.

Method: The study develops a statistical framework for ACA in classification problems, mathematically modeling the effectiveness of collectives based on size and goal alignment with limited knowledge about other groups.

Result: Quantitative bounds are established to measure the effectiveness of multiple collectives influencing a classifier, with simulations showing practical relevance for climate adaptation efforts in smart cities.

Conclusion: The paper provides new tools for understanding ACA in complex multi-group settings, demonstrating how model behavior can be steered collectively even with limited information.

Abstract: As learning systems increasingly shape everyday decisions, Algorithmic Collective Action (ACA), i.e., users coordinating changes to shared data to steer model behavior, offers a complement to regulator-side policy and corporate model design. Real-world collective actions have traditionally been decentralized and fragmented into multiple collectives, despite sharing overarching objectives, with each collective differing in size, strategy, and actionable goals. However, most of the ACA literature focuses on single collective settings. To address this, we propose the first comprehensive statistical framework for ACA with multiple collectives acting on the same system. In particular, we focus on collective action in classification, studying how multiple collectives can influence a classifier's behavior. We provide quantitative statistical bounds on the success of the collectives, considering the role and the interplay of the collectives' sizes and the alignment of their goals. We make such bounds computable by each collective with only partial knowledge of other collectives' sizes and strategies. Finally, we numerically illustrate our framework on simulations inspired by interventions for climate adaptation in smart cities, demonstrating the usefulness of our bounds.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [787] [A Behavioral Framework for Data-Driven Modeling of Nonlinear Systems in Vector-Valued Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07052)
*Boya Hou,Maxim Raginsky*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper generalizes Jan Willems' behavioral approach to nonlinear discrete-time systems using vector-valued RKHS and applies it to data-driven modeling without explicit system identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenge of data-driven modeling of discrete-time nonlinear systems without needing explicit system identification.

Method: It uses Jan Willems' behavioral theory extended to vector-valued RKHS and links it to minimum-norm interpolation and subspace identification methods.

Result: The framework successfully generalizes and integrates behavioral theory with RKHS-based methods for effective data-driven modeling of these systems.

Conclusion: This work provides a robust framework for modeling and analyzing nonlinear systems directly from data, leveraging the strengths of RKHS and behavioral system theory.

Abstract: We generalize Jan Willems' behavioral approach to a class of discrete-time nonlinear systems in a vector-valued reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS). Apart from linear time-invariant systems, this class covers nonlinear systems modeled by Volterra series and their autoregressive variants, as well as systems admitting Hammerstein-type state-space realizations. We apply the proposed framework to the problem of data-driven modeling of such systems, i.e., when simulation or control objectives for an unknown system are carried out without an explicit system identification step. To that end, we link the behavioral approach to two data-driven modeling methods in a vector-valued RKHS: (1) minimum-norm interpolation and (2) subspace identification.

</details>


### [788] [Interactive Trajectory Planning with Learning-based Distributionally Robust Model Predictive Control and Markov Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07768)
*Erik Börve,Nikolce Murgovski,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani,Leo Laine*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper examines interactive trajectory planning under uncertainty, proposing a PAC learning-based DR-MPC framework to improve decision-making by considering both learning model errors and sample counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenges of trajectory planning arise due to uncertainty in the decisions of surrounding agents, requiring better control strategies for the ego-agent.

Method: The approach includes learning the decision distribution, solving a Stochastic Model Predictive Control (SMPC) problem, and leveraging PAC learning combined with Distributionally Robust (DR) optimization.

Result: The framework enables adaptive decision-making that interpolates between robust MPC and omnipotent SMPC, depending on sample availability.

Conclusion: Using a PAC learning-based DR-MPC framework offers a practical method to address trajectory planning uncertainty, enhancing robustness and adaptivity.

Abstract: We investigate interactive trajectory planning subject to uncertainty in the decisions of surrounding agents. To control the ego-agent, we aim to first learn the decision distribution and solve a Stochastic Model Predictive Control (SMPC) problem. To account for errors in the learned distribution, we show that it is possible to utilize Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) learning in combination with Distributionally Robust (DR) optimization to obtain a solution which accounts for the errors induced by the learning model. The results indicate that our PAC learning-based DR-MPC framework provides a method to interpolate between a robust MPC and an omnipotent SMPC, based on the available number of samples.

</details>


<div id='physics.med-ph'></div>

# physics.med-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [789] [Overcoming data scarcity through multi-center federated learning for organs-at-risk segmentation in pediatric upper abdominal radiotherapy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06820)
*Mianyong Ding,Maximilian Knoll,Semi Harrabi,Martine van Grotel,Annemieke S. Littooij,Max van Noesel,Jens-Peter Schenk,Marry M. van den Heuvel-Eibrink,Geert O. Janssens,Matteo Maspero*

Main category: physics.med-ph

TL;DR: The paper evaluates federated learning (FL) for pediatric organs-at-risk segmentation, showing improved cross-center performance and robustness over locally trained models for CT images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underperformance of deep learning-based OAR auto-contouring models trained on adult data when applied to pediatric cases, and the challenges of developing pediatric-specific models due to data scarcity and fragmentation across centers.

Method: FL was employed using an nnU-Net architecture for OAR segmentation across two European medical centers without direct data sharing. Performance was compared to local models using metrics like DSC, with a focus on robustness and error cases.

Result: The FL model achieved competitive local performance and better cross-center results, improving metrics like DSC over local models and demonstrating robustness to patient orientations and reduced false positives.

Conclusion: Federated learning enhances cross-center performance of pediatric-specific OAR segmentation models while preserving data privacy, demonstrating its real-world feasibility and benefits in radiotherapy workflows.

Abstract: Deep learning-based organs/structures-at-risk(OARs) auto-contouring models can improve radiotherapy workflows, but models trained on adult data often underperform in pediatric patients. Developing robust pediatric-specific models is hindered by data scarcity and fragmentation across centers. Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training without the need for data sharing. We evaluated the feasibility and performance of FL for developing pediatric-specific OAR segmentation models across two European medical centers. Computed tomography (CT) images from pediatric patients from Utrecht and Heidelberg with a renal tumor or abdominal neuroblastoma were retrospectively collected and locally processed. An nnU-Net-based framework segmented 19 OARs using local and FL schemes. FL was implemented with secure weight exchange on a cloud storage across institutional firewalls. Performance was assessed using the Dice similarity coefficient (DSC), 95th percentile Hausdorff distance, and mean surface distance. Robustness to patient orientation, false-positive segmentation of surgically removed kidneys, and failure cases were identified. A total of 310 postoperative CTs from 272 patients (105 renal tumors, 167 neuroblastomas) were included. Local models performed well on their respective center data but showed significantly reduced cross-center performance for four to seven of the nine evaluated OARs (DSC). In contrast, the FL model matched local performance for at least seven of nine OARs and achieved the best cross-center results across three metrics, with DSC gains of 0.003-0.007 over local models. FL also maintained stable performance across patient orientations and reduced false-positive kidney segmentations. Real-world FL improves cross-center robustness of CT-based OAR segmentation models in pediatric upper abdominal tumors.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [790] [Physics-Informed Reduced-Order Operator Learning for Hyperelasticity in Continuum Micromechanics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07738)
*Hamidreza Eivazi,Henning Wessels*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method that combines Equilibrium Neural Operator (EquiNO) with QR-based discrete empirical interpolation (Q-DEIM) to significantly speed up and enhance the efficiency of multiscale finite-element simulations for 3D microstructural modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The high computational cost of loss evaluation in physics-informed operator learning limits its practical application in multiscale finite-element simulations, providing the motivation to develop a more efficient approach.

Method: The authors integrate EquiNO, which focuses on reduced displacement-fluctuation and stress representations to enforce periodicity and equilibrium, with Q-DEIM, which uses QR factorization to identify key spatial points for reduced training and evaluation complexity.

Result: The proposed framework reduces training costs by approximately three orders of magnitude and achieves speed-ups of $10^3$ to $10^4$ for homogenization tasks, while providing accurate interpolation and extrapolation of stress fields and homogenized stresses.

Conclusion: The method offers an efficient and accurate surrogate modeling framework for 3D microstructure simulations, making full-batch second-order optimization and reduced computational cost feasible, even for complex scenarios.

Abstract: Physics-informed operator learning is an attractive candidate for surrogate modeling of microstructures, especially in multiscale finite-element simulations. Its practical use, however, is often limited by the high cost of loss evaluation. We address this bottleneck by combining the Equilibrium Neural Operator (EquiNO) with the QR-based discrete empirical interpolation method (Q-DEIM). EquiNO learns only the modal coefficients of reduced displacement-fluctuation and first Piola-Kirchhoff stress representations built from periodic and divergence-free bases, thereby enforcing periodicity and mechanical equilibrium by construction. Q-DEIM then identifies a small set of spatial points through a column-pivoted QR factorization of the stress basis and restricts constitutive evaluations during training to these points alone. This makes full-batch second-order optimization practical for three-dimensional representative volume elements (RVEs). Homogenized first Piola-Kirchhoff stresses are recovered directly from the offline-averaged reduced stress modes, without the need to reconstruct the full stress field at inference time. We validate the framework on two three-dimensional finite-strain hyperelastic RVEs. Q-DEIM reduces the per-step training cost by roughly three orders of magnitude relative to full-field loss evaluation, while reduced homogenization achieves speed-up factors of order $10^3$ to $10^4$ over direct full-field computations. Despite relying on only a small number of offline snapshot loading paths for basis construction, the method accurately interpolates and extrapolates both microscopic stress fields and homogenized stresses, with prediction quality improving systematically as more snapshots are added.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [791] [PersonaGest: Personalized Co-Speech Gesture Generation with Semantic-Guided Hierarchical Motion Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07252)
*Junchuan Zhao,Qifan Liang,Ye Wang*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: PersonaGest introduces a novel framework for co-speech gesture generation that ensures improved semantic coherence and personalization by disentangling content and style through a semantic-guided RVQ-VAE and advanced transformer models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current co-speech gesture generation methods lack the ability to encode semantic structure into motion representations and fail at disentangling content from style, leading to suboptimal results in semantic alignment and personalization.

Method: PersonaGest uses a two-stage approach. Stage 1 employs semantic-guided RVQ-VAE to disentangle motion content and gestural style using a Semantic-Aware Motion Codebook (SMoC) and contrastive learning. Stage 2 uses a Masked Generative Transformer for content generation and cascaded Style Residual Transformers for style control.
